EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary remained over the area which produced multiple rounds of torrential rainfall form heavy thunderstorms from July 9th through July 10th. CAPE values of 2500 to 3500 J/kg and PWAT values of near 1.8 allowed heavy thunderstorm development to produce rainfall rates of 2-3 inches per hour at times. There were parts of the area that receive anywhere from 3-5 inches of rain in a several hour period with some spots seeing upwards of 8 inches. This allowed for the issuance of a localized Flash Flood Emergency for portion of the Lower Hudson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video of water rushing down Wixon Pond Road and East Lake Blvd in Carmel, NY.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Record and near record low temperatures in the 20s during the early morning of May 18th produced a hard freeze across the valley locations of northern NY. This led to a damaging, potentially devastating freeze for several crops as they were in advanced budding stages. Fruit trees like apple peach and blueberries, grape vines and strawberry plants suffered great losses. The main losses were in the lower elevations where an abnormally warm April and early May led to an early growing season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A hard freeze with temperatures in the 20s occurred across much of the area for several hours in the morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms, some with torrential rainfall affected portions of Essex County on July 14. Two to locally five inches of rain fell in a some areas, leading to a few reports of flash flooding and road washouts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms trained across the affected area on the evening of July 14. Radar estimates and ground observations showed that between two and five inches of rain fell, leading to scattered reports of flash flooding. The most notable reports came from the Schroon Lake and Severance area where several reports of flooded roads and washouts occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms affected portions of eastern New York during the afternoon and early evening hours of July 24. A few storms produced heavy rainfall, leading to isolated reports of flash flooding in Essex County, NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms trained across portions of southern Essex County, NY during the afternoon of July 24, 2023. Radar estimates and ground observations showed between two and four inches of rain fell in the affected area, leading to a few reports of flash flooding, most notably in the Paradox, NY area where a few roads were reported washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front near the area forced torrential thunderstorms in a tropical airmass resulting in widespread flash flooding in NY. The first round of thunderstorms occurred early in the morning on July 16th. Another round of thunderstorms occurred later in the afternoon. The tropical airmass had CAPE values 1500 - 2000 J/kg with PWATs 2.2-2.4 inches. The torrential thunderstorms produced rainfall rates as much as 3 inches an hour. Rainfall amounts totaled a widespread 2-4 inches across Long Island with localized spots seeing as much as 5 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding on Southern State Parkway westbound at Exit 44W with all lanes closed.

CAPE values of 2500 to 3500 J/kg and PWAT values of near 1.8 allowed heavy thunderstorm development to produce rainfall rates of 2-3 inches per hour at times. There were parts of the area that receive anywhere from 3-5 inches of rain in a several hour period with some spots seeing upwards of 8 inches. This allowed for the issuance of a localized Flash Flood Emergency for portion of the Lower Hudson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tuxedo park golf club house and surrounding property took on water from rising water levels of nearby lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front near the area forced torrential thunderstorms in a tropical airmass resulting in widespread flash flooding in NY. The first round of thunderstorms occurred early in the morning on July 16th. Another round of thunderstorms occurred later in the afternoon. The tropical airmass had CAPE values 1500 - 2000 J/kg with PWATs 2.2-2.4 inches. The torrential thunderstorms produced rainfall rates as much as 3 inches an hour. Rainfall amounts totaled a widespread 2-4 inches across Long Island with localized spots seeing as much as 5 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portion of Gilbert Street in the village of Monroe closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of low pressure tracked west to east along a stationary boundary, south of New England during the overnight of February 22nd into February 23rd. The storm was a quick mover with about 6-8 hours of snowfall that accumulated 3 to 8 inches across the region, the heaviest in the southern Adirondacks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A general 5 to 8 inches of snow fell across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved from the Ohio River Valley on Wednesday 1/25 to upstate New York and northern New England on Thursday 1/26. Meanwhile an area of low pressure developed along the associated boundary with the parent low across the tri-state region and moved across eastern New England.||Snow overspread northern New York during the afternoon and evening hours of January 25th, heaviest during the evening hours before a lull in the steadiest precipitation occurred during the nighttime hours. Light snow and snow showers redeveloped and moved across northern New York Friday morning and continued through the afternoon.||Snowfall amounts across northern New York ranged from 4 to 10 inches with the heavier amounts the St. Lawrence Valley in St. Lawrence and the Adirondacks of Clinton county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 4 to 7 inches with the higher totals in the Adirondacks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought snow and areas of wintry mix to Central New York on the 22nd. On the East Coast, a secondary low formed, which helped bring in colder air into the region on the 23rd and allowed the mixed precipitation transition back to all snow. Counties that observed less wintry mix were able to have higher snowfall accumulations. The highest totals were east of I-81 where up to a foot of snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 4 and 8 inches.

An arctic cold front passed through the region during the morning of February 3, 2023. Strong cold air advection along with a tightening pressure gradient produced high winds over Southeastern New York during the afternoon and evening hours of this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 132 PM Local Standard Time, a measured wind gust of 62 mph was recorded at a mesonet station (Bayville Station) at an elevation of 41 feet above ground level, located at Matinecock Point in Glen Cove, NY.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front passed through the region during the morning of February 3, 2023. Strong cold air advection along with a tightening pressure gradient produced high winds over Southeastern New York during the afternoon and evening hours of this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 66 mph was recorded at a mesonet station located in Eatons Neck, NY at 623 PM Local Standard Time at an elevation of 71 feet. Additionally, a trained spotter reported a large downed tree at the corner of Norwood and New York Avenues in Port Jefferson, NY at 645 PM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front passed through the region during the morning of February 3, 2023. Strong cold air advection along with a tightening pressure gradient produced high winds over Southeastern New York during the afternoon and evening hours of this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 61 mph was recorded at a mesonet station located at the Point O Woods Yacht Club, 1 mile ENE of Ocean Beach, NY at 710 PM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front passed through the region during the morning of February 3, 2023. Strong cold air advection along with a tightening pressure gradient produced high winds over Southeastern New York during the afternoon and evening hours of this day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front passed through the region during the morning of February 3, 2023. Strong cold air advection along with a tightening pressure gradient produced high winds over Southeastern New York during the afternoon and evening hours of this day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a downed tree, 14 inches in diameter, across Cromwell Road, 2 miles NW of Monroe, NY, at 344 PM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracking across the region brought some mixed precipitation, including freezing rain, to portions of the Adirondacks on February 9, 2023. Slippery travel conditions led to a fuel truck to overturn near Old Forge causing the road to close.||Key impacts: vehicle accidents, road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy road conditions caused a fuel truck to overturn on South Shore Road near Old Forge. The road closed as a result.

Some area schools were delayed or closed as a result of the snow.||Key impacts: school delays and closures.

A pair of low pressure systems tracked along the New York/Pennsylvania state line bringing widespread sleet and freezing rain across much of western New York. Significant icing occurred resulting in scattered downed tree branches and nearly 20,000 without power at the height of the storm. Selected ice totals included 0.71 inch at Buffalo Airport, 0.55 inch at Avon, and 0.5 inch at Scottsville, Warsaw, Le Roy and Elma Center.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Broadalbin Baptist Church was struck by lightning causing some damage to the steeple and some of the electrical equipment.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding up to a foot deep was reported on Oneida Street near Red Hill Road. Road closed to traffic.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was surrounding a residence on Sauquoit Street. Flooding progressively became worse during the night with residents requesting evacuation assistance. Water was around 3 feet deep in the home.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding reported on NY State Route 5 between French Road and Old Bristol Road. Authorities requested closure of the State Highway.

A quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) moved through Central New York, which was accompanied by strong wind gusts. Ahead of this system, scattered thunderstorms developed. Many of these storms had strong wind gusts as well. There were multiple reports of trees and wires down from the cellular storms and the QLCS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NY Mesonet Cohocton station measured 69 mph gust, which is 60 knots.

A line of convective showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of a passing cold front in Central New York. These storms became severe thanks to high shear being present on a cool February day. Strong winds knocked down trees and wires, mainly in Broome County. There were also some reports of 3/4 hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief but powerful Arctic airmass moved across the region on February 3-4, 2023 which brought dangerously low wind chill values to much of the region along with strong wind gusts. An Arctic cold front passed across the region during the evening hours on February 2 accompanied by snow showers and snow squalls. Snow squalls were confined only to the Adirondacks. Thereafter, very cold air gradually advected into the region on February 3 into the morning hours on February 4.||Air temperatures gradually fell throughout the day on February 3 with readings down to the single digits above zero to 10 to 20 below zero by the late-afternoon hours. Many locations dropped to between 10 and 25 degrees below zero with a few pockets in the Adirondacks nearing 40 below zero by the early morning hours on February 4. Portions of the mid-Hudson Valley only fell to between 5 and 10 degrees below zero. On February 4, a record low temperature of -13F was tied at Albany while a record low temperature of -24F was set at Glens Falls. Poughkeepsie was short of their daily record low of -10F by only falling to -7F.||In addition to the cold, this Arctic air mass brought strong winds across the region during the daytime hours on February 3 into the overnight hours before easing on February 4. Peak gusts between 35 and 50 mph were common across most areas. A few localized areas had a peak wind gust greater than 50 mph. A few downed trees and power lines occurred which resulted in a few power outages. The area with the highest amount of power outages was in Ulster County.||The combination of very cold air and strong winds resulted in wind chill values much lower than the actual air temperature. The lowest wind chills ranged from 20 below zero across portions of the mid-Hudson Valley to near 50 below zero across portions of the Adirondacks. For many, the last time it was this cold was back in February 2016.||Warming centers opened areawide to offer assistance for those that needed shelter from the cold. Some area schools closed or were delayed as a result of the cold.||Key impacts: damage to trees and power lines, power outages, school delays and closings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed across the road at Hawthorn Road and Rosehill Boulevard.

A line of convective showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of a passing cold front in Central New York. These storms became severe thanks to high shear being present on a cool February day. Strong winds knocked down trees and wires, mainly in Broome County. There were also some reports of 3/4 hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree and wired down near the intersection of County Highway 23 and Budine Road near Sidney Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold air flowing across Lake Ontario within a deep upper level trough resulted in locally heavy lake effect snow bands impacting central Jefferson and western Lewis counties. The event lasted 18 hours. Due to the snow and localized whiteout conditions, a multicar pile-up including four tractor trailers, occurred on Interstate 81 just south of Watertown, NY. Selected snowfall totals included 17.1 inches near Copenhagen, 13.0 inches in Lorraine, and 9.8 inches near Adams.

A pair of low pressure systems tracked along the New York/Pennsylvania state line bringing widespread sleet and freezing rain across much of western New York. Significant icing occurred resulting in scattered downed tree branches and nearly 20,000 without power at the height of the storm. Selected ice totals included 0.71 inch at Buffalo Airport, 0.55 inch at Avon, and 0.5 inch at Scottsville, Warsaw, Le Roy and Elma Center.

A quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) moved through Central New York, which was accompanied by strong wind gusts. Ahead of this system, scattered thunderstorms developed. Many of these storms had strong wind gusts as well. There were multiple reports of trees and wires down from the cellular storms and the QLCS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed a line of thunderstorms with strong wind gusts and some large hail, from west to east through several counties of Central New York. There were some reports of downed trees and power lines. Ahead of the main line of storms, a tornadic storm developed in Sullivan County. An NWS survey rated the tornado as an EF-2. Surveyed areas included multiple downed trees and structures that sustained damage including a couple of old barns that were destroyed. Fortunately, there were no injuries or fatalities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong disturbance triggered showers and thunderstorms over west-central NY including Yates and Cayuga counties. One of the storms became severe and produced an 85 mph microburst in the vicinity of Penn Yan. Damage from this microburst included a destroyed barn and several snapped/fallen trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a large tree in a backyard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought accumulating snow to Central New York on the night of the 27th. Snowfall was heavy for brief periods of time. While snow continued for much of the overnight hours, snow then transitioned to rain by the afternoon hours on the 28th. The highest snowfall totals were in the Catskills, where over 8 inches fell. Elsewhere, snowfall totals fell short of warning criteria but were enough to result in some minor impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 3 and 6 inches.

There were some reports of downed trees and power lines. Ahead of the main line of storms, a tornadic storm developed in Sullivan County. An NWS survey rated the tornado as an EF-2. Surveyed areas included multiple downed trees and structures that sustained damage including a couple of old barns that were destroyed. Fortunately, there were no injuries or fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down north of Jeffersonville, along Callicoon Center Road near Laundry Brook, damaging trees. The tornado then moved north, passing through a roadless area between Sander Road and Wahl Road, before crossing Hessinger and Lare Road, snapping several trees and causing minor structural damage. The tornado continued north, crossing John Dietz Road, producing a large blowdown of hardwood trees, and damaging skylights in a nearby residence. Damage in this area rated between EF0 and EF1.||The worst damage, particularly to structures, occurred on a farm and nearby residence on either side of Hahn Road. Severe damage to historic barns, a garage, and outbuildings occurred. A 180 foot truss tower collapsed, completely bending into three sections. A nearby house received seemingly minor damage, but frame damage may have occurred as interior walls were reportedly cracked, and doors unable |to close. A large blowdown of mixed trees also occurred in this area. Damage ranged from EF1 to EF2 in this area. ||The tornado continued north, causing another hardwood blowdown off of Gulf Road, with mostly tree damage along nearby Dutch Hill Road. The tornado then passed through Roscoe, NY, with more sporadic uprooted trees, and lifted as it moved into Rockland, NY.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief but powerful Arctic airmass moved across the region on February 3-4, 2023 which brought dangerously low wind chill values to much of the region along with strong wind gusts. An Arctic cold front passed across the region during the evening hours on February 2 accompanied by snow showers and snow squalls. Snow squalls were confined only to the Adirondacks. Thereafter, very cold air gradually advected into the region on February 3 into the morning hours on February 4.||Air temperatures gradually fell throughout the day on February 3 with readings down to the single digits above zero to 10 to 20 below zero by the late-afternoon hours. Many locations dropped to between 10 and 25 degrees below zero with a few pockets in the Adirondacks nearing 40 below zero by the early morning hours on February 4. Portions of the mid-Hudson Valley only fell to between 5 and 10 degrees below zero. On February 4, a record low temperature of -13F was tied at Albany while a record low temperature of -24F was set at Glens Falls. Poughkeepsie was short of their daily record low of -10F by only falling to -7F.||In addition to the cold, this Arctic air mass brought strong winds across the region during the daytime hours on February 3 into the overnight hours before easing on February 4. Peak gusts between 35 and 50 mph were common across most areas. A few localized areas had a peak wind gust greater than 50 mph. A few downed trees and power lines occurred which resulted in a few power outages. The area with the highest amount of power outages was in Ulster County.||The combination of very cold air and strong winds resulted in wind chill values much lower than the actual air temperature. The lowest wind chills ranged from 20 below zero across portions of the mid-Hudson Valley to near 50 below zero across portions of the Adirondacks. For many, the last time it was this cold was back in February 2016.||Warming centers opened areawide to offer assistance for those that needed shelter from the cold. Some area schools closed or were delayed as a result of the cold.||Key impacts: damage to trees and power lines, power outages, school delays and closings.

Poughkeepsie was short of their daily record low of -10F by only falling to -7F.||In addition to the cold, this Arctic air mass brought strong winds across the region during the daytime hours on February 3 into the overnight hours before easing on February 4. Peak gusts between 35 and 50 mph were common across most areas. A few localized areas had a peak wind gust greater than 50 mph. A few downed trees and power lines occurred which resulted in a few power outages. The area with the highest amount of power outages was in Ulster County.||The combination of very cold air and strong winds resulted in wind chill values much lower than the actual air temperature. The lowest wind chills ranged from 20 below zero across portions of the mid-Hudson Valley to near 50 below zero across portions of the Adirondacks. For many, the last time it was this cold was back in February 2016.||Warming centers opened areawide to offer assistance for those that needed shelter from the cold. Some area schools closed or were delayed as a result of the cold.||Key impacts: damage to trees and power lines, power outages, school delays and closings.

An arctic airmass entered northern New York during the early morning hours of February 3rd (Friday) and continued through the evening hours of February 4th (Saturday). ||Daytime actual temperature readings Friday were 10 to 20 below zero with brisk west winds creating wind chills of 30 to 40 below zero. Overnight lows Friday night were 20 to 35 below zero with minimum wind chill values of 30 to 45 below zero and it wasn't until Saturday afternoon when actual air temperatures surpassed zero in spots but developing south winds still produced sub-zero wind chills.||Numerous schools and businesses closed as well as outdoor events, including ski resorts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerously cold temperatures and wind chills impacted the area from early Friday morning through Saturday evening. The coldest was Friday night with low temperatures 20 to 30 below zero and wind chills 30 to 45 below zero.||Some specific low temperatures included; Saranac at -30, Chazy at -29, Ellenburg at -23 and Plattsburgh at -22.|Some specific extreme wind chill values included; -51 at Ellenburg, -46 at Saranac, -44 at Chazy and -40 in Plattsburgh.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak high pressure and a marginally cold air mass was locked into the northeast with blocking to the north across Eastern Canada, while low pressure over the Midwest moved east and redeveloped off the Northern Mid Atlantic coast. This led to the first widespread accumulating snowfall across the CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread 6+ inch snow reports came in across the county. Trained spotters reports included 6.7 inches from Monroe, 6.4 inches in Walden, 8.1 inches from Highland Mills, and 7.0 inches in Newburgh. Cocorahs reports included 8.5 inches in Port Jervis and 6.5 inches in Warwick. General public reports included 7.5 inches in Chester and Pine Bush, 7.0 inches in New Windsor, and 6.3 inches in Cornwall-on-Hudson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak high pressure and a marginally cold air mass was locked into the northeast with blocking to the north across Eastern Canada, while low pressure over the Midwest moved east and redeveloped off the Northern Mid Atlantic coast. This led to the first widespread accumulating snowfall across the CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow was reported across the county. Snowfall reports included 6.5 inches at 2W Putnam Valley, 6.0 inches from Cold Spring, and 5.0 inches from COOP at Carmel Hamlet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak high pressure and a marginally cold air mass was locked into the northeast with blocking to the north across Eastern Canada, while low pressure over the Midwest moved east and redeveloped off the Northern Mid Atlantic coast. This led to the first widespread accumulating snowfall across the CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the northern half of the county. A Cocorahs report had 6.5 inches in Katonah, a COOP report came in with 6.0 inches in Shrub Oak, a trained spotter reported 6.0 inches in White Plains, and public reports of 6.0 inches at 2 NE Somers and 5.7 inches at Croton-on-Hudson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak high pressure and a marginally cold air mass was locked into the northeast with blocking to the north across Eastern Canada, while low pressure over the Midwest moved east and redeveloped off the Northern Mid Atlantic coast. This led to the first widespread accumulating snowfall across the CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the county into the morning rush hour. Snowfall reports of 6.1 inches in Stony Point via Cocorahs, 5.9 inches 1.7 SSW Spring Valley via Cocorahs, and 5.7 inches at 0.6 SE New Hempstead via Cocorahs. Also a trained spotter reported 5.6 inches at 1 WSW New City.  Most of these reports came in likely after some compaction of the snow took place as temperatures were marginal and right near freezing.

A brief but powerful Arctic airmass moved across the region on February 3-4, 2023 which brought dangerously low wind chill values to much of the region along with strong wind gusts. Thereafter, very cold air gradually advected into the region on February 3 into the morning hours on February 4.||Air temperatures gradually fell throughout the day on February 3 with readings down to the single digits above zero to 10 to 20 below zero by the late-afternoon hours. Portions of the mid-Hudson Valley only fell to between 5 and 10 degrees below zero. A quasi-linear convective system (QLCS) moved through Central New York, which was accompanied by strong wind gusts. Ahead of this system, scattered thunderstorms developed. Many of these storms had strong wind gusts as well. There were multiple reports of trees and wires down from the cellular storms and the QLCS.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree onto a minivan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong disturbance triggered showers and thunderstorms over west-central NY including Yates and Cayuga counties. One of the storms became severe and produced an 85 mph microburst in the vicinity of Penn Yan. Damage from this microburst included a destroyed barn and several snapped/fallen trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: First location surveyed was a horse barn with the north side open and it looked like the building was perfectly exposed to the strong winds from the north-northwest to blow the building apart with debris spread to a 155 to 160 degree direction. Looking at post in the ground the mud was soft and waterlogged so it likely did not take as much force to destroy the building so a lower wind estimate was used at 80 mph. ||To the west along City Hill Road there were several medium to large pines that had |either been snapped or uprooted also in a 155 to 160 degree direction. Many of the uprooted trees had a shallow root ball and the hole left by the root ball in the ground was filled with water. To the north along Ridge Road, power poles were either tilting or snapped. The power poles were thinner and older so a lower wind estimate was used resulting in the 85 mph rating. ||Multiple pine trees were also snapped or uprooted at several homes along Ridge Road as well, falling in the 155 to 160 degree direction as well. One home had a chimney that toppled over. Talking to a couple home owners that were home at the time of the |microburst, the stories matched up with strong winds and heavy rain occurring at the same time resulting in white out conditions similar to a blizzard. The stories as well as all the damage and fallen trees leaning in the same direction away from the core of |the storm on radar at the time of the damage means that this was likely a microburst. The Penn Yan Mesonet station from the NYS  mesonet system was on the very edge of the microburst and recorded a gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level weather disturbance tapped into an unusually moist airmass to produce heavy rain with embedded thunderstorms in the Finger Lakes region of New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous basements were flooded in the immediate Seneca Falls area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass entered northern New York during the early morning hours of February 3rd (Friday) and continued through the evening hours of February 4th (Saturday). ||Daytime actual temperature readings Friday were 10 to 20 below zero with brisk west winds creating wind chills of 30 to 40 below zero. Overnight lows Friday night were 20 to 35 below zero with minimum wind chill values of 30 to 45 below zero and it wasn't until Saturday afternoon when actual air temperatures surpassed zero in spots but developing south winds still produced sub-zero wind chills.||Numerous schools and businesses closed as well as outdoor events, including ski resorts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerously cold temperatures and wind chills impacted the area from early Friday morning through Saturday evening. The coldest was Friday night with low temperatures 20 to 30 below zero and wind chills 30 to 45 below zero.||Some specific low temperatures included; Saranac at -30, Chazy at -29, Ellenburg at -23 and Plattsburgh at -22.|Some specific extreme wind chill values included; -51 at Ellenburg, -46 at Saranac, -44 at Chazy and -40 in Plattsburgh.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak high pressure and a marginally cold air mass was locked into the northeast with blocking to the north across Eastern Canada, while low pressure over the Midwest moved east and redeveloped off the Northern Mid Atlantic coast. This led to the first widespread accumulating snowfall across the CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The majority of snow reports ranged from 4 to 5 inches across the southern portion of the county. A trained spotter reported 5.3 inches in Hartsdale, 5.0 inches in Peekskill, and 4.5 inches in Yonkers. Public reports included 5.0 inches in Armonk, 4.8 inches at 1 WSW New Rochelle, with a Cocorahs report of 5.3 inches from Armonk. Thus, just short of reaching warning criteria.

This led to the first widespread accumulating snowfall across the CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cocorahs observers reported 3.5 inches in Manhasset Hills, Herricks, and at 0.2 SSE Albertson which is at the southern border of zone.  The public reported 5.1 inches in Glen Cove.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 5.0 inches in Centereach, 5.0 inches in Smithtown, 4.5 inches in Farmingville, along with a COOP observer reporting 4.2 inches in Mt. Sinai. An NWS Employee reported 4.2 inches in Stony Brook, and Broadcast Media reported 3.5 inches in South Huntington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an upper-level disturbance and nearby surface low and frontal boundary resulted in isolated showers and thunderstorms across portions of eastern New York on July 4, 2023. A few of these thunderstorms became severe producing downed trees and wires. A more concentrated area of damage occurred from the Rensselaer/Columbia County line southward to Styvesant. A NWS Storm Survey confirmed a straight line wind event occurred with winds estimated up to 100 mph causing widespread damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were downed on Route 147 between Galway and Glenville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper low dropped across the lower Great Lakes with a stripe of moderate to heavy snow across western New York. The heaviest snow fell near the Pennsylvania state line over Chautauqua County. Selected snowfall amounts include 9.1 inches at Dewittville, 8.0 inches at Mayville, and 7.7 inches at Gerry.

A weakening low gave way to a stronger coastal low. Heavy snow impacted areas mainly east of Lake Ontario. The greatest snowfall occurred at higher elevations on the Tug Hill Plateau with Constableville measuring 9.6 inches and Highmarket seeing a total of 7.4 inches.

A deepening coastal low brought snow again to the region, this time more widespread from the northern Finger Lakes to the eastern Lake Ontario region. The heaviest snow occurred during the daytime and evening hours of the 14th. Selected snowfall totals included 16.0 inches at Constableville, 15.2 inches at Highmarket, 14.0 inches at Mexico, and 8.0 inches at Wolcott.

An unseasonably cold air mass poured across the Great Lakes setting up heavy lake effect snow. The heaviest snow occurred east of Lake Ontario from the evening hours of the 18th through the morning hours on the 19th. The most snow fell on the higher terrain of the Tug Hill Plateau. Selected snow amounts include 11.0 inches at Montague, 10.0 inches at Lowville and Constableville, and 7.6 inches at West Carthage.

Strong low pressure tracking across the lower Great Lakes to Quebec and a cold front brought a period of strong downslope winds along Lake Erie over far southwest New York during the daytime hours on the 25th. The more widespread strong and damaging winds from this event occurred from the evening of the 25th into the very early morning hours of the 26th. The strongest wind gusts observed were 69 mph at the Niagara Falls Airport and Rochester Airport and 68 mph at the Buffalo airport.

A cold front extending from a strong upper level low moved across the area during the evening hours of the 16th. Ahead of the front, building instability on an unseasonably warm day resulted in a few severe thunderstorms over far western New York, including the Buffalo Metro. A severe wind gust of 59 mph was measured at the Buffalo Airport during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed in Lancaster.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary and low pressure system located to the west and east of eastern New York was the focus for rounds of showers and thunderstorms each day September 7-9. Strong to severe thunderstorms were more widespread on September 7-8. These storms resulted in numerous reports of tree and powerline damage, as well as thousands without power and several road closures. Some school districts across Columbia County delayed classes or closed on September 8 as a result of the severe thunderstorms on September 7.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 61 mph gust was measured at the Schodack New York State Mesonet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passing by to the northwest pushed a pair of cold fronts across the area. As the first front crossed the region during the afternoon of April 1st, a line of severe thunderstorms that developed upstream swept across the area producing wind gusts over 60 mph and widespread wind damage. Another round of strong non-thunderstorm winds then occurred as the second cold front crossed the region late in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed on a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front extending from a strong upper level low moved across the area during the evening hours of the 16th. Ahead of the front, building instability on an unseasonably warm day resulted in a few severe thunderstorms over far western New York, including the Buffalo Metro. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system produced an area of showers and embedded thunderstorms across eastern New York on July 2, 2023. One thunderstorm became severe during the evening hours downing numerous trees and an electrical wire in the Town of Hyde Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and one electrical wire were downed along North Quaker Lane in the Town of Hyde Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked up the East Coast bringing moderate to locally heavy rainfall across the region on April 30, continuing into the overnight hours on May 1. Rainfall amounts were generally in the 1 to 2 inch range with some localized areas receiving 3 inches. This rainfall was enough to result in some minor flooding across portions of eastern New York, mostly ponding of water on roads or flooding in poor drainage areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor poor drainage flooding and standing water on roadways was reported near the Village of Catskill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of torrential, convective rainfall affected portions of west central Clinton, and eastern Franklin Counties during the early morning and afternoon hours of July 2, 2023. Flooding and flash flooding, some significant, occurred in this area, particularly in the Ellenburg, NY area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second round of torrential rainfall affected the region during the afternoon of the 2nd. Additional flash flooding was reported, most notably at the Blue Haven Campground where significant flooding renewed. Evacuations were needed again after campers returned to sites previously flooded earlier that morning. Other significant flooding occurred in Ellenburg where Routes 190 and 11 intersect. Some bridge and residence damage was noted here.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of heavy rainfall affected portions of northeastern New York from late afternoon of the 9th through the early morning hours on the 11th. During the roughly 36-hour period, two to as much as five inches of rain affected these counties in several bursts. This led to scattered areas of flash flooding, some significant to severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of rain fell across the affected area during the late evening and early morning hours of July 10-11. This led to scattered areas of flash flooding, most notably in the towns of Chilson, Olmsteadville and Ironville where several roads were reported damaged or washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several clusters of training showers and thunderstorms, some with torrential rainfall affected portions of Clinton County, NY on the afternoon of July 7, 2023. Areas around Altona, NY and Dannemora, NY were affected by flash flooding after several inches of rain was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clinton County Emergency Mgmt. reported several road closures across the county, most notably in the Altona, NY and Dannemora, NY area. Reliable observations and radar estimates suggest 2-4 inches of rain fell in the area. The Military Turnpike (Route 190) along with Miner Farm Road and Duley Road were noted roads impacted by flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an upper-level disturbance and nearby surface low and frontal boundary resulted in isolated showers and thunderstorms across portions of eastern New York on July 4, 2023. A few of these thunderstorms became severe producing downed trees and wires. A more concentrated area of damage occurred from the Rensselaer/Columbia County line southward to Styvesant. A NWS Storm Survey confirmed a straight line wind event occurred with winds estimated up to 100 mph causing widespread damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was leaning on wires at Oakbrook Manor along Route 9W in Ravena.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary and low pressure system located to the west and east of eastern New York was the focus for rounds of showers and thunderstorms each day September 7-9. Strong to severe thunderstorms were more widespread on September 7-8. These storms resulted in numerous reports of tree and powerline damage, as well as thousands without power and several road closures. Some school districts across Columbia County delayed classes or closed on September 8 as a result of the severe thunderstorms on September 7.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed in multiple locations in the Town of Clermont.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Already saturated grounds from 1-3 inches of rain the previous days set the stage for a localized flash flooding event to unfold on April 30th when an additional 2-3 inches of rainfall fell over a period of several hours. A mesoscale moderate to locally heavy rain band with rainfall rates of 0.5 to 1 per hour set up over the NYC metro area during the afternoon of April 30th when a low pressure system was developing to the south of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: People stranded in vehicles on Cross Island Parkway and Bell Blvd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passing by to the northwest pushed a pair of cold fronts across the area. As the first front crossed the region during the afternoon of April 1st, a line of severe thunderstorms that developed upstream swept across the area producing wind gusts over 60 mph and widespread wind damage. Another round of strong non-thunderstorm winds then occurred as the second cold front crossed the region late in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed on Eelpot Road between Maxfield Road and Route 21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of torrential, convective rainfall affected portions of west central Clinton, and eastern Franklin Counties during the early morning and afternoon hours of July 2, 2023. Flooding and flash flooding, some significant, occurred in this area, particularly in the Ellenburg, NY area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Additional flash flooding affected portions of east central Franklin County, NY in the Bellmont Center area during the afternoon hours on the 2nd. This flooding was tied closely to the larger flash flooding event to the immediate east in the Ellenburg/Lyon Mountain area of Clinton County. Numerous roads were reported to be either closed, or impacted by high water in this area.

Several rounds of torrential, convective rainfall affected portions of west central Clinton, and eastern Franklin Counties during the early morning and afternoon hours of July 2, 2023. Flooding and flash flooding, some significant, occurred in this area, particularly in the Ellenburg, NY area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of heavy rain affected portions of Clinton County from early morning to mid afternoon on July 2, 2023. This event incorporates the first flash flooding occurring during the early morning of the 2nd. Numerous road washouts occurred with several residences experiencing heavy flood damage. The Blue Haven Campground and Ranch Side mobile home park in Ellenburg experienced severe flooding. At Blue Haven, over 200 people were evacuated with at least three campers destroyed and 2 injuries.

A severe wind gust of 59 mph was measured at the Buffalo Airport during the evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary remained over the area which produced multiple rounds of torrential rainfall form heavy thunderstorms from July 9th through July 10th. CAPE values of 2500 to 3500 J/kg and PWAT values of near 1.8 allowed heavy thunderstorm development to produce rainfall rates of 2-3 inches per hour at times. There were parts of the area that receive anywhere from 3-5 inches of rain in a several hour period with some spots seeing upwards of 8 inches. This allowed for the issuance of a localized Flash Flood Emergency for portion of the Lower Hudson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Woman found dead after being swept away by flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent south to southwest winds with gusts of 25 to 35 kt around Bermuda high pressure brought 3 to 4 ft breaking surf and a high risk of rip currents to the local beaches Thursday, and moderate to high on Friday. In addition, hot and humid conditions had large crowds flocking to area beaches. Two rip current fatalities occurred in these conditions at non lifeguarded beaches on consecutive days 7/27 and 7/28 at NYC beaches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two brothers went into the water in a red flagged area at Coney Island Beach on Thursday 7/27 in Brooklyn. The beach had signs saying Danger Drowning Risk from Rip Currents, and no lifeguards were on duty in that area. Good samaritans rescued one boy, but the other was not found. His body washed up on Saturday 7/29.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracked up the East Coast bringing moderate to locally heavy rainfall across the region on April 30, continuing into the overnight hours on May 1. Rainfall amounts were generally in the 1 to 2 inch range with some localized areas receiving 3 inches. This rainfall was enough to result in some minor flooding across portions of eastern New York, mostly ponding of water on roads or flooding in poor drainage areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mapletown Road, Shunk Road, and White Road were closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved into the Northeast, bringing accumulating snow to Central New York. During the overnight hours on the 3rd, the snow transitioned to a wintry mix of rain, freezing rain, and snow. This wintry mix helped keep totals lower across most of the region. The wintry mix did transition back to snow in the morning hours on the 4th. With the heaviest snow just east of the region, isolated areas in the far eastern portions of Delaware and Otsego Counties received up to 9 inches, with up to 7 inches in Northern Oneida County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 3 and 9 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed off the Carolina coast during the late afternoon/evening hours of March 13th and reached offshore of southeastern Massachusetts by the morning of March 14th and then slowly meandered in the Gulf of Maine until the morning of March 15th before exiting away from New England.||Snow began across portions of northern New York just after midnight on March 14th with snowfall rates increasing to more than an inch (up to 2 inches) per hour during the morning and afternoon hours before gradually diminishing but still accumulating until the early morning hours of March 15th.  Brisk north winds gusting 35-45 mph during the night of March 14th, especially in the Champlain valley accounted for some traveling and power issues.||Snowfall totals ranged from 6 to 12 inches in the Champlain Valley and along the International border with Canada to 10 to 18 inches for much of the rest of Vermont with the south and central mountain communities receiving greater than 2 feet.||The wet nature of the snowfall led to scattered to numerous power outages with more than 10,000 customers without power in Essex county. In addition, dozens of roads were closed due to vehicle accidents and downed trees and utility lines as well as many schools closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations of a heavy, wet variety totaled 10 to 20+ inches in Essex county with the lower values in the valley. This led to numerous damaged trees and utility lines with power outages.||Some specific totals include; 30 inches in Schroon Lake, 28 inches in North Hudson/Mineville, 18 inches in Newcomb with around 10 inches in Wilmington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow moved over eastern New York Friday evening (March 3rd) from southwest to northeast. Snow was moderate to at times heavy over the area Friday evening into Friday night. At times, snow mixed with sleet/freezing rain especially over areas south of I-90 (i.e. mid-Hudson Valley). Snow persisted through Friday night before tapering to snow showers Saturday and eventually coming to an end Saturday afternoon/evening.||Snowfall totals ranged from 6 to 12 inches across most of the area except 3-6 inches across the western Mohawk Valley, mid-Hudson Valley and across Columbia, Rensselaer and southern Washington counties.||The weight of the snow resulted in some power outages especially across Albany, Schenectady, Saratoga and Warren counties.||Key Impacts: Power Outages, Travel Limitations.

Snow persisted through Friday night before tapering to snow showers Saturday and eventually coming to an end Saturday afternoon/evening.||Snowfall totals ranged from 6 to 12 inches across most of the area except 3-6 inches across the western Mohawk Valley, mid-Hudson Valley and across Columbia, Rensselaer and southern Washington counties.||The weight of the snow resulted in some power outages especially across Albany, Schenectady, Saratoga and Warren counties.||Key Impacts: Power Outages, Travel Limitations.

Multiple rounds of thunderstorms moved through Central New York throughout daytime hours. Many storms became severe with strong wind gusts being the main hazard. Two NWS surveys confirmed strong microbursts. Both microbursts resulted in widespread damage that included damage to structures. Unfortunately on this day, three roofers were struck by lightning after taking shelter under a tree while waiting for a storm to pass. One of them succumbed to their injuries a few days after the incident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down multiple trees and wires around the area.

An anomalously strong upper low and surface high overhead resulted in a major cold snap eastern New York the night of May 17th into the morning of May 18th. Strong radiational cooling from clear skies resulted in widespread overnight low temperatures in the mid 20s to lower 30s. This resulted in significant impacts to crops/vegetation across the area. A disaster declaration was made for eastern New York due to the extreme cold and crop damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Note: Damage estimates will be determined in the Fall of 2023 based on actual crop harvest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought accumulating snowfall to Central New York on the 10th and 11th. Snowfall totals were generally under a half foot, but a handful of counties were on the higher end of advisory criteria (4 to 7 inch range). Yates County was the only county that observed heavy snowfall of warning criteria, with multiple reports near 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 4 and 7 inches.

An anomalously strong cold snap for mid May standards resulted in some crop damage as temperatures overnight Monday morning of May 15th dropped into the 30s across eastern New York. The coldest locales saw overnight low temperatures fall below freezing. Widespread frost and freeze was reported across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Note: Damage estimates will be determined in the Fall of 2023 based on actual crop harvest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system moved into Central New York on the 13th. Snow continued throughout the day on the 14th and was heavy at times with snowfall rates around or greater than one inch per hour. Snow eventually came to an end on the 15th as the storm moved out of the region. Total snowfall across Central New York varied with just an inch or two in some locations while others received over two feet. Elevation played a factor for the amounts. For many, this was the most snowfall observed from one storm during the 2022-2023 winter season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 8 and 31 inches, highest about 6 miles north of Fleischmanns which measured 31.1 inches.

A powerful Nor'Easter resulted in widespread moderate to heavy wet snow and gusty winds across eastern New York March 13-March 15, 2023. ||Anywhere from 15 to 30 inches of snow with locally higher amounts in excess of 30 inches fell over the SW Adirondacks and eastern Catskills. Valley locations received lesser amounts with totals generally ranging between 7 to 15 inches. A few locations across the western Mohawk Valley and mid-Hudson Valley received less than 6 inches of snow.||Numerous downed trees and powerlines resulted in widespread power outages. According to the National Grid, approximately 141,000 customers lost power within the state of New York with most occurring within our county warning area. Some locations were without power for at least 1 or 2 days. Several warming stations opened to assist those without power. Overall, this event led to the closing of many school districts, and resulted in chain up laws and bans of empty tractor trailers and/or tandems on some area interstates. A State of Emergency was issued for several jurisdictions as a result of the storm. ||Key Impacts: Power Outages, School Closures, Car Accidents, Travel Restrictions.

Overall, this event led to the closing of many school districts, and resulted in chain up laws and bans of empty tractor trailers and/or tandems on some area interstates. A State of Emergency was issued for several jurisdictions as a result of the storm. ||Key Impacts: Power Outages, School Closures, Car Accidents, Travel Restrictions.

An anomalously strong upper low and surface high overhead resulted in a major cold snap eastern New York the night of May 17th into the morning of May 18th. Strong radiational cooling from clear skies resulted in widespread overnight low temperatures in the mid 20s to lower 30s. This resulted in significant impacts to crops/vegetation across the area. A disaster declaration was made for eastern New York due to the extreme cold and crop damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Note: Damage estimates will be determined in the Fall of 2023 based on actual crop harvest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow moved over eastern New York Friday evening (March 3rd) from southwest to northeast. Snow was moderate to at times heavy over the area Friday evening into Friday night. At times, snow mixed with sleet/freezing rain especially over areas south of I-90 (i.e. mid-Hudson Valley). Snow persisted through Friday night before tapering to snow showers Saturday and eventually coming to an end Saturday afternoon/evening.||Snowfall totals ranged from 6 to 12 inches across most of the area except 3-6 inches across the western Mohawk Valley, mid-Hudson Valley and across Columbia, Rensselaer and southern Washington counties.||The weight of the snow resulted in some power outages especially across Albany, Schenectady, Saratoga and Warren counties.||Key Impacts: Power Outages, Travel Limitations.
Gusty winds associated with a line of snow showers and embedded snow squalls resulted in reduced visibilities as well as a few downed trees and wires on the evening of March 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Note: Damage estimates will be determined in the Fall of 2023 based on actual crop harvest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful Nor'Easter resulted in widespread moderate to heavy wet snow and gusty winds across eastern New York March 13-March 15, 2023. ||Anywhere from 15 to 30 inches of snow with locally higher amounts in excess of 30 inches fell over the SW Adirondacks and eastern Catskills. Valley locations received lesser amounts with totals generally ranging between 7 to 15 inches. A few locations across the western Mohawk Valley and mid-Hudson Valley received less than 6 inches of snow.||Numerous downed trees and powerlines resulted in widespread power outages. According to the National Grid, approximately 141,000 customers lost power within the state of New York with most occurring within our county warning area. Some locations were without power for at least 1 or 2 days. Several warming stations opened to assist those without power. Overall, this event led to the closing of many school districts, and resulted in chain up laws and bans of empty tractor trailers and/or tandems on some area interstates. A State of Emergency was issued for several jurisdictions as a result of the storm. ||Key Impacts: Power Outages, School Closures, Car Accidents, Travel Restrictions.

A Miller B type storm brought rain which changed over to heavy wet snow across Interior SE NY. The heaviest snow totals were mostly driven by elevation as temperatures were right at freezing. Most places closer to the coast and towards the metro changed to snow but most places had an inch or less mostly on colder surfaces.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals averaged over 6.0 inches across the county, although amounts varied greatly with some western locations generally receiving less being on the outer edge of the precipitation shield when colder air arrived. Trained spotters reported 15.5 inches at 1 WNW Monroe and 6.1 inches at Salisbury Mills. Public reports included 9.4 inches in Chester and 6.5 inches at 2 SW Greenwood Lake. Cocorahs reports included 8.5 inches in Port Jervis, but only 2.2 inches in Walden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Miller B type storm brought rain which changed over to heavy wet snow across Interior SE NY. The heaviest snow totals were mostly driven by elevation as temperatures were right at freezing. Most places closer to the coast and towards the metro changed to snow but most places had an inch or less mostly on colder surfaces.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The northern half of the zone had snowfall totals at or exceeding 6 inches of heavy wet snow. A trained spotter reported 7.0 inches in Peekskill, with a public report of 6.3 inches at 3 ESE Peekskill. The public also reported 7.1 inches at 1 N North Castle. A report of 4.0 inches came in early the following morning at Shrub Oak 0f 4.0 inches but this was after hours of compaction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Miller B type storm brought rain which changed over to heavy wet snow across Interior SE NY. The heaviest snow totals were mostly driven by elevation as temperatures were right at freezing. Most places closer to the coast and towards the metro changed to snow but most places had an inch or less mostly on colder surfaces.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy wet snow fell across Putnam County resulting in a impactful heavy wet snow into the morning commute. Broadcast media reported 11.9 inches at 3 N Putnam Valley. A Public report came in with 12.0 inches at 1 WSW Lake Carmel. Fire Department and Rescue reported 17.0 inches at 4 NNE Fahnestock State Park.  Cocorahs reported 10.5 inches in Cold Spring and Amateur radio reported 6.4 inches in Mahopac. A trained spotter also reported 13 inches in Kent via social media. Accumulations appeared to be greatly impacted by elevation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed off the Carolina coast during the late afternoon/evening hours of March 13th and reached offshore of southeastern Massachusetts by the morning of March 14th and then slowly meandered in the Gulf of Maine until the morning of March 15th before exiting away from New England.||Snow began across portions of northern New York just after midnight on March 14th with snowfall rates increasing to more than an inch (up to 2 inches) per hour during the morning and afternoon hours before gradually diminishing but still accumulating until the early morning hours of March 15th.  Brisk north winds gusting 35-45 mph during the night of March 14th, especially in the Champlain valley accounted for some traveling and power issues.||Snowfall totals ranged from 6 to 12 inches in the Champlain Valley and along the International border with Canada to 10 to 18 inches for much of the rest of Vermont with the south and central mountain communities receiving greater than 2 feet.||The wet nature of the snowfall led to scattered to numerous power outages with more than 10,000 customers without power in Essex county. In addition, dozens of roads were closed due to vehicle accidents and downed trees and utility lines as well as many schools closed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals were generally 6 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating helped the environment become unstable enough for severe thunderstorms that were kicked off by a frontal system. The storms developed east of I-81 in Central New York and produced damaging wind gusts and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down trees on State Highway 51.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong cold snap for mid May standards resulted in some crop damage as temperatures overnight Monday morning of May 15th dropped into the 30s across eastern New York. The coldest locales saw overnight low temperatures fall below freezing. Widespread frost and freeze was reported across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Note: Damage estimates will be determined in the Fall of 2023 based on actual crop harvest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an upper-level disturbance and nearby surface low and frontal boundary resulted in isolated showers and thunderstorms across portions of eastern New York on July 4, 2023. A few of these thunderstorms became severe producing downed trees and wires. A more concentrated area of damage occurred from the Rensselaer/Columbia County line southward to Styvesant. A NWS Storm Survey confirmed a straight line wind event occurred with winds estimated up to 100 mph causing widespread damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed on King Road and Route 67. One tree fell onto a house.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches were downed near the corner of Regina Lane and Bylina Lane in the City of Amsterdam.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cocorahs observer reported 3.5 inches in Armonk. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front with a feed of rich moisture resulted in widespread moderate to heavy rain and a few instances of mainly road flooding from late in the afternoon on the 12th through early evening hours of the 12th. The heaviest rain and flooding reports received were centered across the Finger Lakes. No lightning was observed with the heavy rain. Some of the heaviest rain reports included; 2.95 inches at Clifton Springs, 2.71 inches in Canandaigua, 2.27 inches at Pultneyville, 2.23 inches at Williamson and 1.96 inches at Sodus Point.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 364 was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed within the proximity of an upper-level low pressure system off the coast of New England. Hail fell out of many of these storms and ranged from pea size (around 0.25 inches) to as large as ping-pong balls (1.50 inches). In some locations, hail fell long enough to accumulate on ground surfaces.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports and pictures of hail around or slightly greater than 1 inch in diameter in Saratoga Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a extended period with no severe weather, active weather returned to the area as a deep upper trough and surface low over the Great Lakes pushed a warm front northward across the region in the morning before a cold front quickly swept in during the afternoon. An embedded impulse along with building instability ahead of the front supported severe thunderstorms most of the afternoon. The majority of reports were received east of Lake Erie, along the southern shore of Lake Ontario and across the Finger Lakes region. In addition to damaging winds and large hail, flash flooding was reported in Chautauqua county near Jamestown and Falconer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of one inch hail was received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms moved through Central New York throughout daytime hours. Many storms became severe with strong wind gusts being the main hazard. Two NWS surveys confirmed strong microbursts. Both microbursts resulted in widespread damage that included damage to structures. Unfortunately on this day, three roofers were struck by lightning after taking shelter under a tree while waiting for a storm to pass. One of them succumbed to their injuries a few days after the incident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down multiple trees done on State Route 7.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms moved through Central New York throughout daytime hours. Many storms became severe with strong wind gusts being the main hazard. Two NWS surveys confirmed strong microbursts. Both microbursts resulted in widespread damage that included damage to structures. Unfortunately on this day, three roofers were struck by lightning after taking shelter under a tree while waiting for a storm to pass. One of them succumbed to their injuries a few days after the incident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree on Twist Run Road.

Hail fell out of many of these storms and ranged from pea size (around 0.25 inches) to as large as ping-pong balls (1.50 inches). In some locations, hail fell long enough to accumulate on ground surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms ahead and along a cold frontal boundary resulted in widespread downed trees and wires across eastern New York during the afternoon and evening hours of July 13, 2023. Thousands were left without power across the Capital Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported at 563 County Route 24.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that formed over the central Great Lakes during the morning survived and tracked east across western New York by early evening. The most instability was built up just southwest of the region across Ohio and western Pennsylvania which resulted in the most concentrated wind damage occurring over Chautauqua County, while wind damage was more sporadic farther north across western New York. A storm survey was conducted in the following days near Lake Erie State Park in western Chautauqua County, but the damage observed was concluded to be caused by straight line winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree and wires were downed on Maple Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was significant flash flooding including a building collapse and a water rescue in Canandaigua in the late afternoon as some estimates point to a 100 year flood event. A state of emergency was declared for Ontario county. Flash flooding also occurred in portions of Lewis County into the early evening. Numerous roads in these counties were flooded and impassable with some washed out. A weak wave of low pressure stalled along a trough combined with increasing moisture and instability to focus waves of showers and thunderstorms with very heavy rain. The axis of heaviest rain stretched from Ontario County to Lewis County. Rainfall amounts along this axis were uneven, but there were pockets of extreme rainfall with over 5.5 inches near Canandaigua in Ontario county and over 4 inches between Lowville and Croghan in Lewis county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A water rescue occurred on Barnes Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was significant flash flooding including a building collapse and a water rescue in Canandaigua in the late afternoon as some estimates point to a 100 year flood event. A state of emergency was declared for Ontario county. Flash flooding also occurred in portions of Lewis County into the early evening. Numerous roads in these counties were flooded and impassable with some washed out. A weak wave of low pressure stalled along a trough combined with increasing moisture and instability to focus waves of showers and thunderstorms with very heavy rain. The axis of heaviest rain stretched from Ontario County to Lewis County. Rainfall amounts along this axis were uneven, but there were pockets of extreme rainfall with over 5.5 inches near Canandaigua in Ontario county and over 4 inches between Lowville and Croghan in Lewis county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County road 30 closed due to flooding. Evacuations occurring at the Happiness House on Independence Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that formed over the central Great Lakes during the morning survived and tracked east across western New York by early evening. The most instability was built up just southwest of the region across Ohio and western Pennsylvania which resulted in the most concentrated wind damage occurring over Chautauqua County, while wind damage was more sporadic farther north across western New York. A storm survey was conducted in the following days near Lake Erie State Park in western Chautauqua County, but the damage observed was concluded to be caused by straight line winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were downed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in Buffalo completed a storm survey|of damage from a line of thunderstorms which occurred during the|early evening of Thursday July 20, 2023. ||Storm damage surveyed was restricted to numerous, scattered |hardwood and softwood trees beginning in Lake Erie State Park near|Brocton, NY. Several trees were blown down, including a large pine|about 2 feet in diameter, and a large silver maple about 3 feet in|diameter. About a dozen large limbs were also blown down within |the park. The tree damage was sporadic, with most trees in the|park not showing any signs of damage. All of the trees and limbs |were blown down in a uniform direction, towards the east- |northeast in alignment with the track of the line of storms.||Widely scattered tree damage continued along Route 5 near the |Lake Erie shore from Lake Erie State Park to the south side of |Dunkirk, NY with several trees uprooted, and around a dozen large |tree limbs blown down. All of this tree damage was also aligned in|a uniform direction, towards the east-northeast.||All of the tree damage surveyed is consistent with straight line|thunderstorm wind damage, with peak wind gusts estimated near 75|mph based on the number and size of trees and limbs down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and pre-frontal trough approached from the northwest at the surface as an upper-level trough remained over the northeast. Lift from the approaching front combined with a moderately unstable environment and 30-35 knots of bulk shear allowed for a severe storm to develop and product large hail and damaging winds in southeast NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report from mPING.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent shower and thunderstorm activity affected portions of southern St. Lawrence County, NY during the late afternoon and evening hours on August 16, 2023. This led to areas of flash flooding, particularly in the Pitcairn, NY area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent showers and thunderstorms affected the southern portions of St. Lawrence County, NY during the late afternoon and evening hours on August 4, 2023. Radar estimates of between two and five inches of rain fell in this area, particularly in the Town of Pitcairn where emergency management reported multiple road washouts, including along Edwards Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Primary focus for initial thunderstorms was toward St. Lawrence Valley as a cold front approached. However, additional thunderstorms developed in the evening to the southeast of Lake Ontario on the southern flank of a strong upper level disturbance crossing the Ottawa Valley. These storms became severe with ample instability and winds aloft. There was a mix of wind and hail reports. Half dollar sized hail was reported over southern portions of Oswego County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received a public report of large hail through social media with a photo. Report was relayed by broadcast media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system with weak winds and high levels of deep moisture pooling along a weak surface trough resulted in clusters of slow moving thunderstorms over Monroe County during the pre-dawn hours. These storms produced very heavy rain with multiple instances of flash flooding reported across the Rochester Metro. Top rainfall reports included; 3.13 inches at the Rochester Airport, 3.00 inches near Greece and 2.84 inches on the northwest side of Rochester.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Foot of water flowing across Interstate 490.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report on social media with a picture showed roads impassible and a submerged car with damage at the corner of Brooks Ave. and Marilou Dr. in Gates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface low tracked across western NY to the northeast of Lake Ontario from the afternoon to the late evening hours. Building instability and increased wind shear ahead of the low supported clusters of severe thunderstorms beginning late in the afternoon across the northern Finger Lakes. Additional northward moving severe storms that exhibited low-level rotation formed early in the evening in a narrow corridor from central New York to areas east of Lake Ontario. In addition to wind damage and large hail, two tornadoes occurred over northeast portions of Oswego County and southern portions of Lewis County during the evening. Eventually, storm surveys confirmed that one of these tornadoes near West Leyden New York to Turin New York tracked 16 miles with observed damage indicating peak winds up to of 140 mph or EF-3 strength. After the tornadic storms diminished, additional storms with very heavy rain tracked over portions of Oswego and Lewis counties from late evening into the overnight hours. These storms which trained over similar areas produced 3 to 4 inches of rain with multiple roads flooded and a few roads entirely washed out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in Buffalo used Sentinel-2 satellite|imagery and found tornadic damage in Lewis County. A damage scar |was found using difference techniques of satellite imagery. The |scar was found in a wooded area east of North Osceola Road in the |Town of Osceola. A damage survey could not be conducted due to |limited access into the wooded area. The tornado is thus rated|EF-Unknown.

These storms which trained over similar areas produced 3 to 4 inches of rain with multiple roads flooded and a few roads entirely washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system with weak winds and high levels of deep moisture pooling along a weak surface trough resulted in clusters of slow moving thunderstorms over Monroe County during the pre-dawn hours. These storms produced very heavy rain with multiple instances of flash flooding reported across the Rochester Metro. Top rainfall reports included; 3.13 inches at the Rochester Airport, 3.00 inches near Greece and 2.84 inches on the northwest side of Rochester.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car was stuck in a sinkhole along St. Paul Boulevard between Paxon Road and Wimbledon Road. There were widespread reports of standing water on roads from Greece to the western portions of Rochester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a warm front lifted across the region in the morning, the arrival of a strong upper level disturbance and surface cold front late in the day combined with ample instability and wind shear to produce severe thunderstorms to the southeast of Lake Ontario during the evening hours. Several reports of wind damage occurred. One storm that exhibited low-level rotation as it moved in off Lake Ontario to the southwest of Oswego produced wind damage with multiple reports of a rotating wall cloud and even a funnel cloud. However, a later survey of the area by county Emergency Management officials did not reveal any signs of tornadic storm damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were downed near State Route 104.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in Buffalo completed a storm survey in Lewis County for damage that is confirmed to be tornadic from Monday evening. Initial damage from the tornado occurred in the Town of Lewis to the west of West Leyden along Mud Lake Road that included uprooted and snapped hardwood trees, significant damage to two homes that included roof uplift and near total removal, a wall blowout of a garage, and the complete destruction and shift from the foundation of a barn. Damage in the initial portion of the track generally peaked at EF-2. The tornado then continued northward and crossed Fish Creek Road with damage to another home that removed the roof and caused significant damage in the walls of the second level of the home. It also did significant damage to a second barn and snapped many hardwood trees.||After moving north of Fish Creek Road, the tornado crossed a large swamp with evidence of full defoliation of a few hardwood trees in the swamp. Tornadic damage become less continuous north of this point without good road access to the track through swampy land.  Convergent track damage again resumed well southwest of the Town of Turin, hitting the town water tower. The tornado then continued northward as it paralleled West Road. Continuous uprooting, snapping, and occasional defoliation of hard and softwood trees was evident for a long portion of the track until it reached Snow Ridge Ski Resort. Upon reaching Snow Ridge Ski Resort, tree damage increased to snapping of hardwood trees at the rootline and some defoliation. On the ski resort land, all of the chair lift metal cables failed, some chair lift structures were overturned or damaged, and the track of damage greatly widened as the storm continued north- |northeastward. The greatest damage along the entire track of the tornado was from Snow Ridge Ski Resort to the West Wind Motel in Turin. Several structures saw completed roof failures, interior wall failures, window failures, and a one multi-story building was shifted from its foundation with its entire roof removed. The tornado lifted after affecting the West Wind Motel with damage ceasing from this point as it crossed State Route 26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary and low pressure system located to the west and east of eastern New York was the focus for rounds of showers and thunderstorms each day September 7-9. Strong to severe thunderstorms were more widespread on September 7-8. These storms resulted in numerous reports of tree and powerline damage, as well as thousands without power and several road closures. Some school districts across Columbia County delayed classes or closed on September 8 as a result of the severe thunderstorms on September 7.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported an estimated thunderstorm wind gusts of 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface high over the region associated with a 588-591 mb heat dome over the Southeastern U.S. resulted in widespread very warm to hot temperatures across eastern New York September 5-7, 2023. Albany International Airport set a record high temperature on September 6 reaching 93F. While air temperatures climbed into the lower 90s along the river valleys, heat indices climbed into the mid to upper 90s. This warranted a Heat Advisory of a good portion of the forecast area during this time frame.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface high over the region associated with a 588-591 mb heat dome over the Southeastern U.S. resulted in widespread very warm to hot temperatures across eastern New York September 5-7, 2023. Albany International Airport set a record high temperature on September 6 reaching 93F. While air temperatures climbed into the lower 90s along the river valleys, heat indices climbed into the mid to upper 90s. This warranted a Heat Advisory of a good portion of the forecast area during this time frame.

A slow moving low pressure system brought multiple rounds of scattered showers and thunderstorms across eastern New York from August 3 to August 4, 2023. Isolated thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours on August 3 in the system's warm sector producing locally damaging wind gusts, small hail and also a funnel cloud. Additional thunderstorm activity developed ahead of a cold front during the morning hours on August 4, especially across Herkimer County, with activity shifting eastward into the Hudson Valley during the afternoon and evening. Additional wind damage and large hail was observed with these storms as well as localized areas of flooding and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was observed looking westward on Clifton Country Road. No damage was spotted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front near the area forced torrential thunderstorms in a tropical airmass resulting in widespread flash flooding in NY. The first round of thunderstorms occurred early in the morning on July 16th. Another round of thunderstorms occurred later in the afternoon. The tropical airmass had CAPE values 1500 - 2000 J/kg with PWATs 2.2-2.4 inches. The torrential thunderstorms produced rainfall rates as much as 3 inches an hour. Rainfall amounts totaled a widespread 2-4 inches across Long Island with localized spots seeing as much as 5 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: DOT reports flooding and road closure on NY 116 in both directions between North Road and Hunt Lane with all lanes closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system brought multiple rounds of scattered showers and thunderstorms across eastern New York from August 3 to August 4, 2023. Isolated thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours on August 3 in the system's warm sector producing locally damaging wind gusts, small hail and also a funnel cloud. Additional thunderstorm activity developed ahead of a cold front during the morning hours on August 4, especially across Herkimer County, with activity shifting eastward into the Hudson Valley during the afternoon and evening. Additional wind damage and large hail was observed with these storms as well as localized areas of flooding and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed near Elnora.
