EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large limbs down reported by the Central Park Conservancy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front plowed across the lower Great Lakes region during the evening hours pushing a broken line of thunderstorms across western New York. Numerous marine thunderstorm wind reports were received as the line of thunderstorms moved through with even an isolated wind damage report over Niagara County. The cold air behind this front then helped spawn a waterspout late the following afternoon over southeast Lake Ontario.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was downed and a fence was damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system brought multiple rounds of scattered showers and thunderstorms across eastern New York from August 3 to August 4, 2023. Isolated thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours on August 3 in the system's warm sector producing locally damaging wind gusts, small hail and also a funnel cloud. Additional thunderstorm activity developed ahead of a cold front during the morning hours on August 4, especially across Herkimer County, with activity shifting eastward into the Hudson Valley during the afternoon and evening. Additional wind damage and large hail was observed with these storms as well as localized areas of flooding and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed on Bush Road at Mowers Road in the Town of Schuyler.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary moved through Central New York and supported multiple thunderstorms. High instability and shear supported scattered severe thunderstorms containing damaging wind gusts, large hail, and a NWS survey also confirmed a tornado in Delaware County. There were dozens of reports of trees down from other storms, with some trees falling onto occupied vehicles. Fortunately there were no injuries. There were also many reports of large hail with the largest size reported was 1.5 inches, which is about the size of a ping pong ball.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree on State Route 274.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance moving through the upper atmosphere across southern New York combined with an unusually warm and moist airmass to produce several rounds of scattered showers and thunderstorms over Chemung county. A very localized total of 2 to 3 inches of rain fell just to the west of Elmira, NY. The runoff from this heavy rain overwhelmed several small streams near the Horseheads exchange along I-86. The Interstate was severely flooded and remained closed until the morning of the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water from two small creeks rushed rapidly off of a nearby hill and significantly flooded both lanes of I-86 near the Horseheads interchange. The interstate remained closed until the following morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought a period of snow and a wintry mix of precipitation, including freezing rain, to portions of eastern New York beginning in the afternoon hours on November 21. The highest snowfall totals were across portions of the Lake George Saratoga region and the southeastern Adirondacks where 4 to 7 inches of snow fell with a light glaze of ice in the same areas as well as around portions of the Capital District. Precipitation gradually changed over to plain rain as warmer air moved into the region overnight into the morning hours on November 22.||The hazardous travel conditions resulted in a few vehicle accidents across the area.||Key Impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents.

Thunderstorms developed ahead of a slow moving cold front tracking through Western and Central New York during the afternoon and evening. Numerous thunderstorm complexes moved over the same locations in Onondaga county producing up to 6 inches of rain in a short amount of time. Much of the area affected was predominantly urbanized land cover. Flooding of streets and drainage systems occurred for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of I-690 was closed due to water on the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few showers and thunderstorms developed within the warm sector of a low pressure system across eastern New York during the afternoon and evening hours on August 12, 2023. Most of the activity was located across the mid-Hudson Valley (Ulster and Dutchess counties) where thunderstorms produced several instances of wind damage and some large hail. Localized flash flooding also occurred with these storms. A few other thunderstorms produced some localized wind damage closer to the proximity of a warm front across Herkimer and Warren counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires were downed blocking part of Route 55A near Napanoch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary and low pressure system located to the west and east of eastern New York was the focus for rounds of showers and thunderstorms each day September 7-9. Strong to severe thunderstorms were more widespread on September 7-8. These storms resulted in numerous reports of tree and powerline damage, as well as thousands without power and several road closures. Some school districts across Columbia County delayed classes or closed on September 8 as a result of the severe thunderstorms on September 7.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed along the Route 22 corridor between Petersburgh and Hoosick Falls.

A band of lake-effect snow off Lake Ontario produced heavy snowfall across the southern Adirondacks from November 27-28, 2023. Snow began during the early afternoon hours on November 27 and continued into the morning hours on November 28. By the afternoon hours on November 28, the band dropped southward with lighter snow showers and flurries continuing until ending later in the evening. Snowfall totals in excess of one foot were observed where the band was most persistent which included portions of Route 28.||Key Impacts: Transportation Delays.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds snapped a large (5 inch diameter) tree branch. The branch landed into a yard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept through Central New York, kicking off numerous thunderstorms. An unstable environment favored scattered severe thunderstorms with damaging wind gusts and large hail. There were multiple reports of 1+ inch sized hail.

A frontal boundary moved through Central New York and supported multiple thunderstorms. High instability and shear supported scattered severe thunderstorms containing damaging wind gusts, large hail, and a NWS survey also confirmed a tornado in Delaware County. There were dozens of reports of trees down from other storms, with some trees falling onto occupied vehicles. Fortunately there were no injuries. There were also many reports of large hail with the largest size reported was 1.5 inches, which is about the size of a ping pong ball.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down multiple trees on Silsbee Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few showers and thunderstorms developed within the warm sector of a low pressure system across eastern New York during the afternoon and evening hours on August 12, 2023. Most of the activity was located across the mid-Hudson Valley (Ulster and Dutchess counties) where thunderstorms produced several instances of wind damage and some large hail. Localized flash flooding also occurred with these storms. A few other thunderstorms produced some localized wind damage closer to the proximity of a warm front across Herkimer and Warren counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed at the intersection of Route 32 and Forest Road near Plattekill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary and low pressure system located to the west and east of eastern New York was the focus for rounds of showers and thunderstorms each day September 7-9. Strong to severe thunderstorms were more widespread on September 7-8. These storms resulted in numerous reports of tree and powerline damage, as well as thousands without power and several road closures. Some school districts across Columbia County delayed classes or closed on September 8 as a result of the severe thunderstorms on September 7.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A road was closed due to downed trees on wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept through Central New York, kicking off numerous thunderstorms. An unstable environment favored scattered severe thunderstorms with torrential rainfall over a short period of time. Rainfall amounts of 3 to 5 inches fell over a concentrated area in under 3 hours contributing to rapid flooding of many roads, ditches and culverts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Parking lots were flooded around the village of Hartwick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornado outbreak occurred across Central New York during the afternoon and evening hours. Strong shear supported multiple rotating thunderstorms. While damaging wind gusts were a hazard, tornadoes were the main concern. Multiple NWS surveys were performed over a couple of days to confirm a total of five tornadoes. Surveyed damage included downed trees and wires as well as structure damage to multiple residences. Fortunately, no one was injured or killed during these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near the intersection of Route 38 and Purvis Road in far eastern Tompkins County and knocked down some trees near the border of Tompkins and Cortland County. It then continued in Cortland County for many miles, finally lifting in a wooded area between West River Road and the Tioughnioga River ||At the time that the tornado touched down in Tompkins County, it was an EF-0, but it became an EF-1 in Cortland County. The majority of damage occurred in Cortland County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornado outbreak occurred across Central New York during the afternoon and evening hours. Strong shear supported multiple rotating thunderstorms. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near the intersection of Route 38 and Purvis Road in far eastern Tompkins County. It then moved northeastward through a mostly wooded area  in Cortland County from Harford to Virgil. The most widespread damage was found along Clute Road where several trees were uprooted and snapped and a large shed/storage building had its roof partially torn off and partially collapsed. The tornado then brought down more trees along a property along Stafford Road before continuing northeastward to West River Road. Several snapped and uprooted trees were observed at residences along this road. The last observed damage was in a wooded area between West River Road and the Tioughnioga River and this is where the tornado lifted.||At the time that the tornado touched down in Tompkins County, it was an EF-0, but it became an EF-1 in Cortland County. The majority of damage occurred in Cortland County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado briefly touched down along the south shore of the East Branch Delaware River near Fishs Eddy causing damage to trees. An eyewitness reported seeing his 75-100 foot hemlock tree twist and snap off about halfway up the trunk. Another eyewitness captured video of a rotating wall cloud in the general vicinity of this |area of damage. The tornado crossed O&W Road, proceeding uphill causing further damage to some trees in the forest and powerlines along Hubbell Hill Road. Due to extremely steep terrain, the evidence of further tornado damage was unobtainable through these wooded areas. Additional tree damage was observed at the intersection of O&W Road and Halls Island Road to the southeast of East Branch. The tornado is believed to have skipped through the mountainous area before lifting over the Beaver Kill. No further damage was noted or reported across the river toward Baxter Mountain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near the intersection of Mountain Road and |Stevens Point Road in Harmony Township northeast Susquehanna County Pennsylvania at 625 PM EDT Monday August 7th 2023. The tornado snapped and |uprooted many trees on Stevens Point Road as the tornado tracked |north-northeast roughly along Stevens Point Road. The tornado then |weakened as it crossed the PA - NY border with only some minor |tree damage along Laurel Lake Road in the Town of Sanford, Broome |County. The tornado then strengthened as it crossed over a hill and |hit Kenyon Hill Road. There was a residence on Kenyon Hill Road in |the Town of Sanford that bore the brunt of the damage with windows |blown out, shingles blown off the roof and numerous trees snapped |and uprooted. A shed was moved about 100 feet with a couple boats on |a pond lofted in the air. The tornado reached its maximum intensity |of 90 mph in this location before lifting over the next hillside.

High instability and shear supported scattered severe thunderstorms containing damaging wind gusts, large hail, and a NWS survey also confirmed a tornado in Delaware County. There were dozens of reports of trees down from other storms, with some trees falling onto occupied vehicles. Fortunately there were no injuries. There were also many reports of large hail with the largest size reported was 1.5 inches, which is about the size of a ping pong ball.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak low pressure systems embedded in a hot and humid airmass over the region contributed to several rounds of heavy rain-producing thunderstorm activity. Isolated locations in Central New York received between 2 to 4 inches of rainfall and experienced flooding of streams, creeks and roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain contributed to flooding of Route 31 along the Tuscarora Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hard freeze occurred over all of western and north central New York on May 18. The freezing temperatures damaged many apple orchards and grape vineyards across the region, especially across the Finger Lakes. Damage to crops and buds for the grape crop is estimated in some areas to be at least 50 percent. Many growers some that have been doing this nearly 40 years said it was the worst frost damage they have ever seen. A preliminary estimate for monetary damages caused by the hard freeze is 250-500K dollars. A sampling of lowest temperatures recorded included 24 degrees in Allegany, 25 degrees in Belmont, 26 degrees in Bethany, Calcium, Honeoye Falls, East Bloomfield,  Hastings and Walworth, 27 degrees in Frewsburg, Copenhagen and York, 28 degrees in Lockport and Middleport along with 29 degrees in East Aurora and Albion.

Dual areas of low pressure moved across NY, southern New England and just southeast of Cape Cod during the early morning hours of November 27th. This brought precipitation into the Adirondacks and Champlain Valley during the late evening hours of November 26th, exiting before dawn on November 27th.||Much of the precipitation fell as rain with marginal temperatures, but a heavy wet snow fell at elevations, mainly above 1000-1500 during the time of greatest precipitation intensity. Heavy wet snow in the Adirondacks of Essex county led to scattered power outages as well as slick, hazardous roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall accumulations ranged from a few inches in the valleys to 6-10 inches at elevations above 2000 feet. The heavy wet snow led to scattered power outages due to bending or broken tree limbs onto power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms associated with shortwave troughs erupted over eastern New York during the afternoon hours on July 24th and July 25th resulting in multiple reports of downed trees and wires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime-sized hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A non-severe storm in Oneida County contained large hail. Hailstones as large as 3/4 inches were observed just east of Griffiss Air Force Base.

A low pressure system brought a period of snow and a wintry mix of precipitation, including freezing rain, to portions of eastern New York beginning in the afternoon hours on November 21. The highest snowfall totals were across portions of the Lake George Saratoga region and the southeastern Adirondacks where 4 to 7 inches of snow fell with a light glaze of ice in the same areas as well as around portions of the Capital District. Precipitation gradually changed over to plain rain as warmer air moved into the region overnight into the morning hours on November 22.||The hazardous travel conditions resulted in a few vehicle accidents across the area.||Key Impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 40 was closed between Guilder Hollow Road and Warren Road due to a crash in the Town of Hartford.

A weak area of low pressure was traveling across the eastern Great Lakes during the early morning hours of July 16th. There was enough marginal instability that showers in the eastern Great Lakes developed into a few strong thunderstorms as they moved north-northeast across Lake Ontario and eastern Ontario province in Canada toward the St. Lawrence Valley of NY. One rogue thunderstorm produced some marginal wind damage in the form of downed trees and branches in the Waddington-Madrid area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and branches downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought a period of moderate snowfall to portions of the Adirondacks during the evening hours on November 26 into the overnight hours on November 27. Snowfall amounts between 3 and 7 inches were common. The snow resulted in some power outages across Hamilton and northern Warren counties. Over 7,000 customers were without power in Warren County.||Key Impacts: power outages.

Multiple rounds of thunderstorms moved through Central New York throughout daytime hours. Many storms became severe with strong wind gusts being the main hazard. Two NWS surveys confirmed strong microbursts. Both microbursts resulted in widespread damage that included damage to structures. Unfortunately on this day, three roofers were struck by lightning after taking shelter under a tree while waiting for a storm to pass. One of them succumbed to their injuries a few days after the incident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three construction workers were struck by lightning in the town of Lincoln while sheltering under a tree and waiting for a storm to pass. Unfortunately, all sustained injuries with one  of them later passing away due to their injuries.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The core of a severe thunderstorm descended right onto Cayuga Lake around |224 PM EDT on July 24, 2023, blasting the immediate eastern shore with a |microburst of 70 to 80 mph winds at Union Springs. Numerous trees had |large branches snapped and a few were uprooted right at the edge of the |lake, also causing downed wires and a power pole. At least two houses, |and one shed, sustained moderate roof damage from large branches falling |on them. Several others had more cosmetic or minor damage from small |branches. There were also downed trees, a broken metal sign, and minor |boat damage at a marina. The property manager there even observed a |miniature seiche of sorts, with the power of the wind causing water to |surge a bit into the marina during the storm, then it flowed back out |shortly after.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water up to 6 inches deep was reported running across Gulf Road and washing out portions of the shoulder and pavement.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm that moved through Tompkins County on the evening|of Monday, July 24th has been determined to have produced a|microburst that destroyed two barns, uprooted trees, and caused a|divergent path of downed trees in the direction of eastward storm|motion. The epicenter of the microburst was most likely at the|intersection of Hayts Road and Sheffield Road in Enfield, NY, where|two barns were lifted off the foundation, mostly destroyed and|displaced northwestward. Progressing eastward on Hayts Road,|uprooted trees and multiple downed trees in the eastward direction|were observed. These damage indicators began to have diverging|directions both northeast- and southeast-ward, indicating the|spread of a wind pool from a microburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front across Central New York and a passing weak disturbance supported scattered thunderstorms. One storm became severe with damaging wind gusts. One motorist was injured when a tree fell on their car.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds knocked down multiple trees along County Highway 12.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree onto a vehicle, injuring the driver.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept through Central New York, kicking off numerous thunderstorms. An unstable environment favored scattered severe thunderstorms with damaging wind gusts and large hail. There were multiple reports of 1+ inch sized hail.

A cold front swept through Central New York, kicking off numerous thunderstorms. An unstable environment favored scattered severe thunderstorms with damaging wind gusts and large hail. There were multiple reports of 1+ inch sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down multiple trees near Lake Moraine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swept through Central New York, kicking off numerous thunderstorms. An unstable environment favored scattered severe thunderstorms with damaging wind gusts and large hail. There were multiple reports of 1+ inch sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a large tree on Higby Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving front and shortwave trough aloft approached from the west. Surface heating led to moderate instability and a severe thunderstorm was able to develop and produce damaging wind gusts and large hail as it moved through southeastern NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of trees down on roads, cars and houses with poles and wires down in multiple locations. Power out for many.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary remained over the area which produced multiple rounds of torrential rainfall form heavy thunderstorms from July 9th through July 10th. CAPE values of 2500 to 3500 J/kg and PWAT values of near 1.8 allowed heavy thunderstorm development to produce rainfall rates of 2-3 inches per hour at times. There were parts of the area that receive anywhere from 3-5 inches of rain in a several hour period with some spots seeing upwards of 8 inches. This allowed for the issuance of a localized Flash Flood Emergency for portion of the Lower Hudson Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two persons trapped in vehicle in flood waters. Fire Department responding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave/convectively induced vort translated out of the Great Lakes Region as a cold front approached at the surface. A line of severe thunderstorms moved through southeast NY and produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree down on home on Cromwell Road in Chester NY.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracking south of the area caused southeasterly winds over southwest NY, resulting in downsloping winds for the Chautauqua Ridge area. Several reports of downed trees and wires were received. The highest wind gust measured was 63 mph at the NYS Mesonet site near Fredonia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were downed in the Findley Lake, Fredonia, Westfield and Sheridan areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first lake effect snow event of the season impacted areas mainly east and southeast of Lake Erie and Lake Ontario. A series of passing cold fronts and troughs helped make this event a longer duration event covering portions of three days. Some of the hardest hit areas received between one and four feet of snow. For the most part, heavy lake effect snow did not occur in Buffalo or Watertown with this event. Some selected higher snowfall totals for this event off of Lake Ontario included 46.2 inches at Highmarket, 33.5 inches near Redfield and 33.0 inches in Osceola. Off of Lake Erie, higher snowfall totals included 23.0 inches in Chautauqua, 22.0 inches in Arcade and 21.0 inches in Randolph.

Some selected higher snowfall totals for this event off of Lake Ontario included 46.2 inches at Highmarket, 33.5 inches near Redfield and 33.0 inches in Osceola. Off of Lake Erie, higher snowfall totals included 23.0 inches in Chautauqua, 22.0 inches in Arcade and 21.0 inches in Randolph.

Lake effect snow developed off of Lake Onatario. The main band mainly impacted Northern Oneida County with heavy snowfall while areas further south received less. This band drifted over portions of the Finger Lakes on the 29th with light snowfall accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 6 and 14 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and pre-frontal trough slowly crossing the area stalled over the Eastern Lake Ontario area. As the front stalled, a few bands of heavy rain developed along the front as an influx of moisture moved in ahead of and along the front.  This resulted in flash flooding overnight in southern Lewis County. Some of the highest rainfall amounts from this event, all from Lewis County, included 3.20 at Chases Lake; 3.14 at Croghan; and 2.11 near Glenfield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Shibley Road was washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy lake effect rain east of Lake Ontario caused flooding to occur in Oswego County. With cooler air aloft over the much warmer lake waters of Lake Ontario and with a vertically stacked low pressure system at the surface and mid-levels in place, a strong lake response developed east of Lake Ontario. This resulted in periods of heavy rain for Oswego County, resulting in flooding issues along County Route 20. Some of the highest rainfall amounts from this event, all from Oswego County, included 2.60 near Minetto; 1.58 near Mexico; and 1.23 near Lacona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred on County Route 20.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface boundary moved through Central New York, kicking off a line of storms across the region. This line of thunderstorms had two portions with one line moving north and another line moving east. With a moisture rich and unstable environment ahead of the boundary, thunderstorms quickly became severe. Damaging wind gusts were the main threat with these storms. The majority of damage reports were for downed trees and utility lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm that formed on the northern edge of an outflow boundary over central Lake Ontario tracked east late in the evening producing prolific lightning out over the lake. This storm reached Jefferson County shortly before midnight and produced isolated wind damage before weakening as it moved farther inland away from Lake Ontario.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed in Ellisburg along State Route 3.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Out ahead of a cold front during the last very warm and humid day of a recent stretch of hot weather, thunderstorms developed mid to late afternoon from central Pennsylvania to central New York. A line of severe storms developed in this area and lifted north across the Finger Lakes and eventually the eastern Lake Ontario region. Several reports of wind damage were received as these storms moved through, especially over Oswego and Lewis counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed in Hastings at State Route 49 and Canterbury Way.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front provided the focus for the development of slow moving heavy thunderstorms on the afternoon of September 8th 2023. The thunderstorms developed in an unstable environment with CAPE values of 3000-4000 J/kg and PWATs near 1.7 - 1.8 inches. These thunderstorms produced rainfall rates of 1-2 inches per hour with a total accumulation of 1-3 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency vehicle being dispatched to respond to flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy thunderstorms developed in an unstable and moist environment on September 11th, 2023 and produced flash flooding in New York. Thunderstorms developed along a stationary front that was draped over the area in an environment with 2000-2500 J/kg of SBCAPE and 1.7-1.9 inches of PWAT. The thunderstorms produced rainfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour and a total of 1-3 inches of rainfall in a couple of hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding reported by DOT with road closure due to flooding on the Saw Mill Parkway at Exit 4A at Rumsey Road in Yonkers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inverted trough extending from a low pressure system over the Western Atlantic provided a focus for persistent heavy convective rain to continually develop over the NYC metro and surrounding areas during much of the day on September 29th, 2023. In an environment with anomalously high moisture given the time of year, PWATs near 1.5-1.7 inches, rounds of heavy rainfall fell over the same areas for more than 12-18 hours. While the rainfall rates were generally 1 inch per hour or less, the persistence in the heavier rainfall resulted in a widespread total of 4-6 inches of rain with JFK receiving almost 9 inches of rain from the event. There were some brief periods of time where rainfall rates peaked at near 2 inches per hour. This resulted in widespread flash flooding across Southeast NY and Southern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several Cars Stranded in flood waters with rescues Underway on Peninsula Blvd near Woodmere Blvd.

A line of severe storms developed in this area and lifted north across the Finger Lakes and eventually the eastern Lake Ontario region. Several reports of wind damage were received as these storms moved through, especially over Oswego and Lewis counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed blocking Hodge Road in the Town of Watson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inverted trough extending from a low pressure system over the Western Atlantic provided a focus for persistent heavy convective rain to continually develop over the NYC metro and surrounding areas during much of the day on September 29th, 2023. In an environment with anomalously high moisture given the time of year, PWATs near 1.5-1.7 inches, rounds of heavy rainfall fell over the same areas for more than 12-18 hours. While the rainfall rates were generally 1 inch per hour or less, the persistence in the heavier rainfall resulted in a widespread total of 4-6 inches of rain with JFK receiving almost 9 inches of rain from the event. There were some brief periods of time where rainfall rates peaked at near 2 inches per hour. This resulted in widespread flash flooding across Southeast NY and Southern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Submerged vehicle in flood waters on Richmond Ave. Fire department responding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface boundary moved through Central New York, kicking off a line of storms across the region. This line of thunderstorms had two portions with one line moving north and another line moving east. With a moisture rich and unstable environment ahead of the boundary, thunderstorms quickly became severe. Damaging wind gusts were the main threat with these storms. The majority of damage reports were for downed trees and utility lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree onto the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary and low pressure system located to the west and east of eastern New York was the focus for rounds of showers and thunderstorms each day September 7-9. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were reported down.

In an environment with anomalously high moisture given the time of year, PWATs near 1.5-1.7 inches, rounds of heavy rainfall fell over the same areas for more than 12-18 hours. While the rainfall rates were generally 1 inch per hour or less, the persistence in the heavier rainfall resulted in a widespread total of 4-6 inches of rain with JFK receiving almost 9 inches of rain from the event. There were some brief periods of time where rainfall rates peaked at near 2 inches per hour. This resulted in widespread flash flooding across Southeast NY and Southern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long Island Railroad service suspended on the Far Rockaway Branch between Valley Stream and Far Rockaway due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms developed to the west of the area along a surface trough in an unstable environment characterized by SBCAPE values of 2500 to 2500 J/kg. These thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts across southeast NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires down and burning on Route 211 East between Hidden Valley Drive and Goshen Turnpike in Scotchtown NY.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms developed to the west of the area along a surface trough in an unstable environment characterized by SBCAPE values of 2500 to 2500 J/kg. These thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts across southeast NY.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary and low pressure system located to the west and east of eastern New York was the focus for rounds of showers and thunderstorms each day September 7-9. Strong to severe thunderstorms were more widespread on September 7-8. These storms resulted in numerous reports of tree and powerline damage, as well as thousands without power and several road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms developed amid a warm and humid air mass ahead of an approaching cold front on July 7, 2023. Some storms became severe downing trees and powerlines. Locally heavy rainfall also resulted in some instances of street flooding and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto a wire near Bolton Landing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a frontal boundary during the afternoon hours on July 29th across eastern New York. One lone thunderstorm tracked across the Mohawk Valley resulting in numerous reports of downed trees and powerlines as well as a couple of flood reports. Prior to the arrival of these thunderstorms, the combination of hot and humid conditions resulted in heat index values between 95 and 100 degrees across all of Dutchess and eastern Ulster counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on Keller Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of rounds of showers and thunderstorms resulted in wind damage and flash flooding over parts of eastern New York on July 16, 2023. One round occurred during the early morning hours which resulted in some downed trees and wires as well as some street flooding and washouts, primarily across the mid-Hudson Valley. The second round moved through during the afternoon leading to some flooding and flash flooding across Saratoga, Washington, Greene and Rensselaer counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Part of State Route 29 was impassable due to reports of water coming across the roadway.

The second round moved through during the afternoon leading to some flooding and flash flooding across Saratoga, Washington, Greene and Rensselaer counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 29 had all lanes closed between Hepatica Way and Hardscrabble Road due to a Flash Flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of rounds of showers and thunderstorms resulted in wind damage and flash flooding over parts of eastern New York on July 16, 2023. One round occurred during the early morning hours which resulted in some downed trees and wires as well as some street flooding and washouts, primarily across the mid-Hudson Valley. The second round moved through during the afternoon leading to some flooding and flash flooding across Saratoga, Washington, Greene and Rensselaer counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 9 NB/SB between Route 67 and Wood Road has all lanes closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level system brought widespread rounds of showers and thunderstorms to eastern New York on July 9-11, 2023. The steadiest and heaviest rainfall occurred during the afternoon hours on July 9, the early morning hours on July 10 and during the evening hours on July 10 into the overnight hours on July 11. Rainfall totals varied greatly from region to region but the hardest hit areas (Hudson Valley and into northeastern Hamilton County) received 3 to 6 inches of rainfall.||Areal and flash flooding was primarily focused across areas along the Hudson River on July 9-10. Damage consisted of closed and washed out roads. A section of Metro-North's railroad tracks were washed out due to flood waters from Poughkeepsie southward.||The combination of heavy rain and beaver dam failures resulted in destruction of roads, bridges and sidewalks across northeastern Hamilton County during the early morning hours on July 11 focused on the areas of Long Lake and Blue Mountain Lake. A State of Emergency was declared for the towns of Long Lake and Indian Lake. New York Governor Kathy Hochul declared a State of Emergency for Hamilton County. Hamilton County Emergency Manager estimated damage to be in the millions of dollars.||In addition to the heavy rainfall, a few severe thunderstorms also developed across portions of the Hudson Valley leading to downed trees and wires. A lightning strike caused an auto body shop to catch fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and debris was over Pleasant Ridge Road near Poughquag.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms ahead and along a cold frontal boundary resulted in widespread downed trees and wires and a few reports of flooding across eastern New York during the afternoon and evening hours of July 13, 2023. One injury was reported as a tree fell onto an occupied vehicle. Thousands were left without power across the Capital Region.||Additional isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed during the evening and early overnight hours on July 14-15, 2023 producing additional reports of hail, wind damage and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of slow-moving, training, and heavy rain producing thunderstorms resulted in a downed tree and flash flooding over parts of eastern New York on July 18. The bulk of the heaviest rain and flooding reports were concentrated in and around the Capital District including the City of Albany, where some cars were stuck in rising flood waters. More than 5,000 residents in Albany County lost power as a result of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down along with wires on Guy Park Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a frontal boundary during the afternoon hours on July 29th across eastern New York. One lone thunderstorm tracked across the Mohawk Valley resulting in numerous reports of downed trees and powerlines as well as a couple of flood reports. Prior to the arrival of these thunderstorms, the combination of hot and humid conditions resulted in heat index values between 95 and 100 degrees across all of Dutchess and eastern Ulster counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down at Route 334 near Common Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an upper-level disturbance and nearby surface low and frontal boundary resulted in isolated showers and thunderstorms across portions of eastern New York on July 4, 2023. A few of these thunderstorms became severe producing downed trees and wires. A more concentrated area of damage occurred from the Rensselaer/Columbia County line southward to Styvesant. A NWS Storm Survey confirmed a straight line wind event occurred with winds estimated up to 100 mph causing widespread damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in Albany, New York surveyed damage west of Route 9 across southwestern Rensselaer County and northwestern Columbia County. Across this area, damage was consistent with straight line winds. There were at least three areas of concentrated damage where a majority of trees were felled. Two were along NY Route 9J north of Styvesant where long segments of the road were completely buried in debris. Another was near the intersection of Ridge Road and Requa Road southeast of Schodack Landing.||Some notable damage included trees down on houses and wires, some siding removed from one house and the metal roof of a barn peeled back. Large fields of corn were flattened and a metal flag pole was bent. One resident reported their wooden furniture blown off the porch and a canvas carport lifted by the wind and deposited about a quarter of a mile away. At the Hook Boat Club, falling trees damaged campers and boats. Additionally, the CSX freight line was shut down in this area due to fallen trees.||Estimated peak winds were 100 mph with a damage path of about 5.4 miles and a width of 1.75 miles.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were reported down at Hardenburgh Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms associated with a shortwave trough and cold front developed and moved over parts of eastern New York during the afternoon and evening hours on July 27th resulting in numerous reports of downed trees and wires across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wires were reported down on High Point Mountain Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a frontal boundary during the afternoon hours on July 29th across eastern New York. One lone thunderstorm tracked across the Mohawk Valley resulting in numerous reports of downed trees and powerlines as well as a couple of flood reports. Prior to the arrival of these thunderstorms, the combination of hot and humid conditions resulted in heat index values between 95 and 100 degrees across all of Dutchess and eastern Ulster counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down blocking both lanes of Bouck Road just south of Widrick Road in the town of Frankfort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of slow-moving, training, and heavy rain producing thunderstorms resulted in a downed tree and flash flooding over parts of eastern New York on July 18. The bulk of the heaviest rain and flooding reports were concentrated in and around the Capital District including the City of Albany, where some cars were stuck in rising flood waters. More than 5,000 residents in Albany County lost power as a result of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a report of a road washout near the intersection of New Salem South Road, New Salem Road and Route 85 in the Town of New Scotland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level system brought widespread rounds of showers and thunderstorms to eastern New York on July 9-11, 2023. The steadiest and heaviest rainfall occurred during the afternoon hours on July 9, the early morning hours on July 10 and during the evening hours on July 10 into the overnight hours on July 11. Rainfall totals varied greatly from region to region but the hardest hit areas (Hudson Valley and into northeastern Hamilton County) received 3 to 6 inches of rainfall.||Areal and flash flooding was primarily focused across areas along the Hudson River on July 9-10. Damage consisted of closed and washed out roads. A section of Metro-North's railroad tracks were washed out due to flood waters from Poughkeepsie southward.||The combination of heavy rain and beaver dam failures resulted in destruction of roads, bridges and sidewalks across northeastern Hamilton County during the early morning hours on July 11 focused on the areas of Long Lake and Blue Mountain Lake. A State of Emergency was declared for the towns of Long Lake and Indian Lake. New York Governor Kathy Hochul declared a State of Emergency for Hamilton County. Hamilton County Emergency Manager estimated damage to be in the millions of dollars.||In addition to the heavy rainfall, a few severe thunderstorms also developed across portions of the Hudson Valley leading to downed trees and wires. A lightning strike caused an auto body shop to catch fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a propane tank and caused an automotive body shop to catch fire.

Thousands were left without power across the Capital Region.||Additional isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed during the evening and early overnight hours on July 14-15, 2023 producing additional reports of hail, wind damage and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down across Route 9G.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms associated with shortwave troughs erupted over eastern New York during the afternoon hours on July 24th and July 25th resulting in multiple reports of downed trees and wires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 15 foot sailboat capsized at Red Hook Dock due to strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of slow-moving, training, and heavy rain producing thunderstorms resulted in a downed tree and flash flooding over parts of eastern New York on July 18. The bulk of the heaviest rain and flooding reports were concentrated in and around the Capital District including the City of Albany, where some cars were stuck in rising flood waters. More than 5,000 residents in Albany County lost power as a result of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed in and around the Gardiner area due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of rounds of showers and thunderstorms resulted in wind damage and flash flooding over parts of eastern New York on July 16, 2023. One round occurred during the early morning hours which resulted in some downed trees and wires as well as some street flooding and washouts, primarily across the mid-Hudson Valley. The second round moved through during the afternoon leading to some flooding and flash flooding across Saratoga, Washington, Greene and Rensselaer counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down across Rivka Road near Saugerties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an upper-level disturbance and nearby surface low and frontal boundary resulted in isolated showers and thunderstorms across portions of eastern New York on July 4, 2023. A few of these thunderstorms became severe producing downed trees and wires. A more concentrated area of damage occurred from the Rensselaer/Columbia County line southward to Styvesant. A NWS Storm Survey confirmed a straight line wind event occurred with winds estimated up to 100 mph causing widespread damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 9J and Knickerbocker Road were closed due to multiple downed trees.

The second round moved through during the afternoon leading to some flooding and flash flooding across Saratoga, Washington, Greene and Rensselaer counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level system brought widespread rounds of showers and thunderstorms to eastern New York on July 9-11, 2023. The steadiest and heaviest rainfall occurred during the afternoon hours on July 9, the early morning hours on July 10 and during the evening hours on July 10 into the overnight hours on July 11. Rainfall totals varied greatly from region to region but the hardest hit areas (Hudson Valley and into northeastern Hamilton County) received 3 to 6 inches of rainfall.||Areal and flash flooding was primarily focused across areas along the Hudson River on July 9-10. Damage consisted of closed and washed out roads. A section of Metro-North's railroad tracks were washed out due to flood waters from Poughkeepsie southward.||The combination of heavy rain and beaver dam failures resulted in destruction of roads, bridges and sidewalks across northeastern Hamilton County during the early morning hours on July 11 focused on the areas of Long Lake and Blue Mountain Lake. A State of Emergency was declared for the towns of Long Lake and Indian Lake. New York Governor Kathy Hochul declared a State of Emergency for Hamilton County. Hamilton County Emergency Manager estimated damage to be in the millions of dollars.||In addition to the heavy rainfall, a few severe thunderstorms also developed across portions of the Hudson Valley leading to downed trees and wires. A lightning strike caused an auto body shop to catch fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A section of Metro-North's railroad tracks were washed out due to flood waters from Poughkeepsie southward.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a frontal boundary during the afternoon hours on July 29th across eastern New York. One lone thunderstorm tracked across the Mohawk Valley resulting in numerous reports of downed trees and powerlines as well as a couple of flood reports. Prior to the arrival of these thunderstorms, the combination of hot and humid conditions resulted in heat index values between 95 and 100 degrees across all of Dutchess and eastern Ulster counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Herkimer County officials reported trees down in Ilion due to strong thunderstorm winds.

Thousands were left without power across the Capital Region.||Additional isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed during the evening and early overnight hours on July 14-15, 2023 producing additional reports of hail, wind damage and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree and wires were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of rounds of showers and thunderstorms resulted in wind damage and flash flooding over parts of eastern New York on July 16, 2023. One round occurred during the early morning hours which resulted in some downed trees and wires as well as some street flooding and washouts, primarily across the mid-Hudson Valley. The second round moved through during the afternoon leading to some flooding and flash flooding across Saratoga, Washington, Greene and Rensselaer counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portion of County Route 113 in Easton was impassable due to significant amounts of water and tree debris over the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level system brought widespread rounds of showers and thunderstorms to eastern New York on July 9-11, 2023. The steadiest and heaviest rainfall occurred during the afternoon hours on July 9, the early morning hours on July 10 and during the evening hours on July 10 into the overnight hours on July 11. Rainfall totals varied greatly from region to region but the hardest hit areas (Hudson Valley and into northeastern Hamilton County) received 3 to 6 inches of rainfall.||Areal and flash flooding was primarily focused across areas along the Hudson River on July 9-10. Damage consisted of closed and washed out roads. A section of Metro-North's railroad tracks were washed out due to flood waters from Poughkeepsie southward.||The combination of heavy rain and beaver dam failures resulted in destruction of roads, bridges and sidewalks across northeastern Hamilton County during the early morning hours on July 11 focused on the areas of Long Lake and Blue Mountain Lake. A State of Emergency was declared for the towns of Long Lake and Indian Lake. New York Governor Kathy Hochul declared a State of Emergency for Hamilton County. Hamilton County Emergency Manager estimated damage to be in the millions of dollars.||In addition to the heavy rainfall, a few severe thunderstorms also developed across portions of the Hudson Valley leading to downed trees and wires. A lightning strike caused an auto body shop to catch fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many branches and a few trees were downed along Route 40 south of Schaghticoke.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of heavy rainfall affected portions of northeastern New York from late afternoon of the 9th through the early morning hours on the 11th. During the roughly 36-hour period, two to as much as five inches of rain affected these counties in several bursts. This led to scattered areas of flash flooding, some significant to severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of rain fell during the evening hours on July 9. This led to areas of significant flash flooding, most notably in the towns of Saranac, Redford and Altona. The town of Altona was hardest hit, with numerous road washouts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying storm moved up the Eastern Seaboard from northern Florida on December 17th, to the coastal Mid Atlantic region on the 18th. This storm produced a strong onshore flow on the 18th that combined with high astronomical tides to cause widespread moderate to locally major coastal flooding and beach erosion along the south shore of Long Island during the late morning and afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to locally major coastal flooding occurred along the south shore of Nassau:||At the Freeport USGS tidal gauge, major floooding of 7.2 ft MLLW occurred at 1236 PM EST. Moderate flooding of 6.5 ft MLLW or more occurred from 1036 AM EST to 130 PM EST. ||At the Reynolds Channel tidal gauge, moderate coastal flooding of 7.0 ft MLLW or more occurred from 1148 AM EST to 1236 PM EST, with a peak water level of 7.3 ft MLLW from 1224 PM EST to 1236 PM EST. ||At the East Rockaway Inlet USGS tidal gauge, moderate flooding of 7.2 ft MLLW or more occurred from 1118 AM EST to 1242 PM EST, with a peak water level of 7.5 ft from 1206 PM EST to 1224 PM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying storm moved up the Eastern Seaboard from northern Florida on December 17th, to the coastal Mid Atlantic region on the 18th. This storm produced a strong onshore flow on the 18th that combined with high astronomical tides to cause widespread moderate to locally major coastal flooding and beach erosion along the south shore of Long Island during the late morning and afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major coastal flooding occurred along the south shore of SW Suffolk, and major beach erosion also occurred along the south shore of Fire Island:||At the Watch Hill/Fire Island USGS tidal gauge, major flooding above the threshold of 4.2 ft MLLW occurred from 142 PM EST to 506 PM EST, with a peak water level of 4.7 ft MLLW from 248 PM EST to 342 PM EST. Moderate flooding of 3.7 ft MLLW or more occurred from 1242 PM EST to 612 PM EST.||At the Lindenhurst USGS tidal gauge, major coastal flooding at the threshold of 4.1 ft MLLW occurred from 124 PM EST to 212 PM EST. Moderate flooding of 3.6 ft MLLW or more occurred from 1148 AM EST to 324 PM EST. ||At the West Sayville USGS tidal gauge, major coastal flooding of 4.1 ft MLLW or more occurred from 118 PM EST to 518 PM EST, with a peak a peak water level of 4.5 ft MLLW from 300 PM EST to 318 PM EST. Moderate flooding of 3.6 ft MLLW or more occurred from 1148 AM EST to 636 PM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure tracked up the East Coast and intensified in the process as it brought widespread moderate to heavy rainfall across eastern New York on December 18, 2023. Rainfall totals were between 2 and 5 inches with the highest amounts across the eastern Catskills. This rain led to both areal and river flooding across the region. Some rivers didn't recede until December 22.||States of Emergency were issued for the Town of Johnsburg and Warren County. Several area schools had an early dismissal on December 18 or delays on December 19 due to the flooding.||A 76-year-old woman died after their vehicle was swept off Cauterskill Road due to flood waters from Catskill Creek after driving around a traffic barrier blocking the road.||Gusty winds accompanied this event with gusts between 35 and 50 mph. This resulted in some localized power outages due to downed trees and wires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of John Joy Road were closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved north along the U.S. east coast and passed through the region during the morning of December 18, 2023. The pressure gradient tightened as the center of the storm approached, producing high winds over Southeastern New York during the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 67 mph was recorded at the Harbor Road mesonet station located in Port Washington, NY at 330 AM Local Standard Time. In addition, a trained spotter reported a fence blown down in Hicksville, NY at 700 AM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved north along the U.S. east coast and passed through the region during the morning of December 18, 2023. The pressure gradient tightened as the center of the storm approached, producing high winds over Southeastern New York during the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NYC Emergency Management relayed a report of a total of 321 instances of downed trees, limbs and hanging limbs across Queens. The time of these instances most likely occurred between 400 AM and 700 AM Local Standard Time based on radar data and nearby ground observations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front ahead of a developing coastal low pressure system positions itself over the area late on December 17th and into December 18th. As the low pressure intensified and approached, significant amounts of moisture focused along the warm front ahead of the coastal low resulting in widespread areas of heavy rainfall that occurred over a 12-18 hour period. PWATs were 1.5-1.7 inches which were near record values. A widespread 2-5 inches of rain fell over the area. This resulted in many areas of flash flooding as well as significant rises in rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding and road closure on NY 303 in both directions in Clarkstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure along the East Coast caused heavy rainfall as far west as the North Country. Heavy rain prompted the issuance of an areal flood warning for portions of Oswego and Lewis counties for general flooding with an emphasis on streams, creeks and rivers. Subsequent flood warnings were issued for both the Moose River and Black River. Three reports were received for flooding from area streams and rivers, all in Lewis County, including flooding near New Bremen, Port Leyden and East Lyonsdale.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of Wildcat Road and River Road closed due to flooding of a small nearby stream.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread rain transitioned to snow as an area of strong low pressure and a potent trough tracked near and over the region. As colder air infiltrated behind a passing cold front, lake enhancement and upsloping along the higher terrain of the Chautauqua Ridge caused heavy snowfall for portions of Chautauqua County. Snowfall for this event included 7.2 inches in Cassadaga.

Low pressure moved north along the Atlantic Seaboard spreading steady and heavy rain into central New York during the overnight hours. Rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches were observed along with a considerable amount of melting snow into area rivers and streams. Widespread flash flooding and river flooding occurred during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of roads is occurring, especially Lakeshore Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front ahead of a developing coastal low pressure system positions itself over the area late on December 17th and into December 18th. As the low pressure intensified and approached, significant amounts of moisture focused along the warm front ahead of the coastal low resulting in widespread areas of heavy rainfall that occurred over a 12-18 hour period. PWATs were 1.5-1.7 inches which were near record values. A widespread 2-5 inches of rain fell over the area. This resulted in many areas of flash flooding as well as significant rises in rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding on NY 9D both directions near Fair Street. All lanes closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front associated with an area of low pressure across Quebec brought a widespread rainfall to eastern New York on December 10, 2023. Colder air behind the front combined with a developing area of low pressure tracking off the East Coast caused rain to change over to wet snow during the overnight hours and into the morning hours on December 11 before gradually ending. After receiving between 1.00 and 2.50 inches of rainfall, snow totals ranged from 4 to 7 inches across portions of the southern Adirondacks and eastern Catskills with generally 3 inches or less elsewhere. Impacts associated with this event were mainly limited to some power outages across Hamilton County.||Key impacts: power outages.

Lake-induced snow showers developed across eastern New York on December 13, 2023, first across areas north of I-90, then slowly drifted southward throughout the day. Within these snow showers, a quick coating to 2 inches of snow fell with some of the heavier snow showers greatly reducing visibility and was accompanied by gusty winds. The arrival of colder air behind the front and snow showers resulted in some roads becoming icy. These snow showers reached the Capital District around the time of the evening commute. A few vehicle accidents were reported due to the hazardous travel conditions.||Key impacts: travel delays, vehicle accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A crash was reported on Route 43 at Lape Road in the Town of North Greenbush.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved north along the Atlantic Seaboard spreading steady and heavy rain into central New York during the overnight hours. Rainfall totals were between 2 and 5 inches with the highest amounts across the eastern Catskills. This rain led to both areal and river flooding across the region. Some rivers didn't recede until December 22.||States of Emergency were issued for the Town of Johnsburg and Warren County. Several area schools had an early dismissal on December 18 or delays on December 19 due to the flooding.||A 76-year-old woman died after their vehicle was swept off Cauterskill Road due to flood waters from Catskill Creek after driving around a traffic barrier blocking the road.||Gusty winds accompanied this event with gusts between 35 and 50 mph. This resulted in some localized power outages due to downed trees and wires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported over Horsenden Road near New Paltz.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved north along the Atlantic Seaboard spreading steady and heavy rain into central New York during the overnight hours. Rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches were observed along with a considerable amount of melting snow into area rivers and streams. Several river gages exceeded posted flood stages with some even exceeding their Moderate flood category levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oneida Creek at Oneida exceeded moderate flood stage and crested at 13.39 feet on December 18th at 4:45 PM. Water up to this level spreads into a mobile home park in Oneida Castle and affects Prospect and Highland Streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved north along the U.S. east coast and passed through the region during the morning of December 18, 2023. The pressure gradient tightened as the center of the storm approached, producing high winds over Southeastern New York during the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 59 mph was recorded at a mesonet station located in Fordham, NY at 450 AM Local Standard Time. In addition, NYC Emergency Management relayed a report of a total of 43 instances of downed trees, limbs and hanging limbs across the Bronx. The time of these instances most likely occurred between 400 AM and 700 AM Local Standard Time based on radar data and nearby ground observations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved north along the U.S. east coast and passed through the region during the morning of December 18, 2023. The pressure gradient tightened as the center of the storm approached, producing high winds over Southeastern New York during the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 64 mph occurred at a mesonet station located at Larchmont Harbor, NY at 500 AM Local Standard Time. A measured wind gust of 58 mph occurred at a mesonet station located at Disbrow Park, NY at 455 AM Local Standard Time. In addition, the Department of Highways reported a tree down on the Hutchinson Parkway, north of Cross County Parkway in Bronxville, NY at 629 AM Local Standard Time.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hoosic River at Eagle Bridge rose above flood stage, reaching moderate flood stage during the late afternoon hours on December 18 to the early morning hours on December 19. Water overflowed onto River Road next to the Buskirk covered bridge as well as County Route 103 between Route 67 and the covered bridge. In addition, houses were flooded near Eagle Bridge. Newspaper reported flood waters partially submerging a truck and trailer in Buskirk. Portions of Buskirk-West Hoosick Road were also closed.

Some rivers didn't recede until December 22.||States of Emergency were issued for the Town of Johnsburg and Warren County. Several area schools had an early dismissal on December 18 or delays on December 19 due to the flooding.||A 76-year-old woman died after their vehicle was swept off Cauterskill Road due to flood waters from Catskill Creek after driving around a traffic barrier blocking the road.||Gusty winds accompanied this event with gusts between 35 and 50 mph. This resulted in some localized power outages due to downed trees and wires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dippikill Road was closed due to flooding. The floodwaters cut off the bridge near Glen Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system initially brought rain which changed over to wet snow from northwest to southeast. Rain changed to snow in the wake of a cold front the evening of the 10th and continued into the early morning of the 11th with higher elevations changing quicker. The steadiest and heaviest snow fell from the late evening hours on the 10th into the predawn hours on the 11th over portions of the Catskills. Snow ended from west to east by mid morning on the 11th. With this event being a heavy wet snow, there were scattered power outages in the southern Catskills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 6 and 14 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lake effect snow developed off of Lake Ontario during the morning of the 13th behind a cold front as winds became west-northwesterly. Snow primarily impacted Northern Oneida County before shifting southward during the evening toward the NYS Thruway corridor and produced lower amounts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated between 6 and 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved north along the U.S. east coast and passed through the region during the morning of December 18, 2023. The pressure gradient tightened as the center of the storm approached, producing high winds over Southeastern New York during the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 59 mph was recorded at a mesonet station located at the College of Staten Island in Staten Island, NY at 520 AM Local Standard Time. A measured wind gust of 66 mph was recorded at a mesonet station located at Robbins Reef Lighthouse at 448 AM Local Standard Time. In addition, NYC Emergency Management relayed a report of a total of 64 instances of downed trees, limbs and hanging limbs across Staten Island. The time of these instances most likely occurred between 400 AM and 700 AM Local Standard Time based on radar data and nearby ground observations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved north along the Atlantic Seaboard spreading steady and heavy rain into central New York during the overnight hours. Rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches were observed along with a considerable amount of melting snow into area rivers and streams. Widespread flash flooding and river flooding occurred during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Prentice Road is closed by flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intensifying storm moved up the Eastern Seaboard from northern Florida on December 17th, to the coastal Mid Atlantic region on the 18th. This storm produced a strong onshore flow on the 18th that combined with high astronomical tides to cause widespread moderate to locally major coastal flooding and beach erosion along the south shore of Long Island during the late morning and afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate coastal flooding occurred along the south shore of Queens:||At the Jamaica Bay (Inwood) USGS tidal gauge, moderate flooding above the threshold of 8.5 ft MLLW occurred from 1154 AM to 1230 PM, with a peak water level of 8.6 ft MLLW from 1200 PM EST to 1212 PM EST.||At East Rockaway Inlet, moderate flooding above the threshold of 7.2 ft MLLW occurred from 1118 AM EST to 1224 PM EST, with a peak water level of 7.5 ft MLLW from 1206 PM EST to 1224 PM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved north along the U.S. east coast and passed through the region during the morning of December 18, 2023. The pressure gradient tightened as the center of the storm approached, producing high winds over Southeastern New York during the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind gust of 58 mph was recorded at a mesonet station located in Shinnecock, NY at 726 AM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved north along the U.S. east coast and passed through the region during the morning of December 18, 2023. The pressure gradient tightened as the center of the storm approached, producing high winds over Southeastern New York during the morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple damage reports included a downed tree blocking the Route 135 northbound exit 6 ramp in Plainedge, NY, at 450 AM Local Standard Time, as reported by the Department of Highways, as well as a power pole down, estimated to occur at around 500 AM Local Standard Time on the road in Carle Place, NY, as reported by a trained spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed over portions of the Southern Tier and Genesee Valley as a strong trough and convective system tracked across the area. The warning was issued for one inch hail, wind gusts to 60 mph and a tornado possible. The storm resulted in a downed tree in the Town of Hume in Allegany County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed in Hume.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure along the East Coast caused heavy rainfall as far west as the North Country. Heavy rain prompted the issuance of an areal flood warning for portions of Oswego and Lewis counties for general flooding with an emphasis on streams, creeks and rivers. Subsequent flood warnings were issued for both the Moose River and Black River. Three reports were received for flooding from area streams and rivers, all in Lewis County, including flooding near New Bremen, Port Leyden and East Lyonsdale.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of Muncy Road and Kotel Road closed due to flooding of a small nearby stream.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved north along the Atlantic Seaboard spreading steady and heavy rain into central New York during the overnight hours. Rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches were observed along with a considerable amount of melting snow into area rivers and streams. Widespread flash flooding and river flooding occurred during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video from social media shows fast moving water rushing down roads in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of thunderstorms moved through Central New York throughout daytime hours. Many storms became severe with strong wind gusts being the main hazard. Two NWS surveys confirmed strong microbursts. Both microbursts resulted in widespread damage that included damage to structures. Unfortunately on this day, three roofers were struck by lightning after taking shelter under a tree while waiting for a storm to pass. One of them succumbed to their injuries a few days after the incident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down multiple trees and wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system moved through Central New York and helped support one severe thunderstorm in Delaware County. A couple of trees were knocked down from the strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds knocked down a tree onto State Route 268.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving front and shortwave trough aloft approached from the west. Surface heating led to moderate instability and a severe thunderstorm was able to develop and produce damaging wind gusts and large hail as it moved through southeastern NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of quarter-sized hail in Peekskill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent shower and thunderstorm activity affected portions of eastern Essex County, NY  during the afternoon of August 16, 2023. The towns of Moriah and Westport received between one and three inches of rain leading to a few areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent shower and thunderstorm activity affected portions of eastern Essex County, NY during the afternoon of August 16, 2023. Radar estimates and observational reports showed that between one and three inches of rain affected this area leading to a few reports of flash flooding, mainly in the towns of Moriah and Westport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving front and shortwave trough aloft approached from the west. Surface heating led to moderate instability and a severe thunderstorm was able to develop and produce damaging wind gusts and large hail as it moved through southeastern NY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Poles and wires down on Orange Avenue in Goshen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid atmospheric disturbance moved over a unstable airmass across northern New York during the afternoon hours of July 4th. Scattered thunderstorms rotated north to southeast across the region, some of them providing localized damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and powerlines downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few weak areas of low pressure traversed New York and New England during the night of December 3rd into the morning of December 4th. Precipitation moved across northern New York during the afternoon of December 3rd and exited during the early morning hours of December 4th.||Precipitation was highly elevated dependent with elevations above 1200-1500 feet witnessing primarily wet snow with lower elevations receiving a mix of ran and wet snow. Greatest snowfall totals were across the Adirondacks of Clinton county with 4 to 8+ inches resulting in some travel and power disruptions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A highly elevation dependent snowfall with 2 to 4 inches in the valleys and 4 to 8 + inches at elevations above 1200-1500 feet. This led to travel and power disruptions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very deep trough across the eastern seaboard allowed a deep, mild, moist flow from the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic ocean to feed into the northeast United States, including VT and NY on December 17th and 18th.||Surface low pressure across Florida on December 17th moved north along the eastern seaboard into New England on December 18th. Mild, moist air delivered 1.5 to 3+ inches of rainfall to northeast NY along with temperatures in the 40s that promoted snowmelt of approximately 1-1.5 inches. Widespread saturated soil inundation flooding such as flooded fields, yards, spilling (flowing) into basements, low-lying roads and streams, eventually into small rivers and eventually into the AuSable River basin.||Much of the flooding was local watersheds and poor drainage but moderate to major flooding was observed in/around the AiuSable River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant headwater flooding across roads, streams within the AuSable basin. The AuSable River at AuSable Forks recorded it's 8th highest crest of record.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front meandered across northern NY during the afternoon and evening hours of December 10th with a weak area of low pressure developing along that front and moving across eastern Massachusetts during the December 11th. ||A mild, wet December 10th slowly turned snowy from west to east and higher elevations to lower elevations during the evening and overnight hours of December 10th before ending by midday on December 11th.  Snow was heaviest in elevations above 1500 feet in western Essex county which led to approximately 2000 customers without power and hazardous travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heavy wet snow fell across the region with lower valleys receiving 3 to 6 inches, while the higher elevations received 5 to 10 inches. Power outages of approximately 2000 customers occurred in Essex county.
